CHANGELOG.html.title: Changelog | MGM Character Controller
CHANGELOG.html.summary: Changelog All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file. The format is based on Keep a Changelog and this project adheres to Semantic Versioning. [0.6.0] - 2026-04-03 Added FlyCam sample — Minimal first-person fly camera scene demonstrating single-state machines, gravity-free movement, split entity/camera rotation, and contact clearing Samples registration — SinglePlayerSample and FlyCamSample registered in package.json Changed PostCollisionImpulse queue — Replaced NativeParallelMultiHashMap with NativeQueue for deterministic ordering; impulses are now sorted before application and the separate clear job is removed Removed ProfilerRecorderMap / LocomotionProfilerHUD — Profiler overlay components removed Fixed Documentation URLs — Updated documentationUrl and changelogUrl in package.json to v0.6.0 Broken doc links and orphaned meta file — Fixed sample README links and removed orphaned Documentation.meta [0.5.0] - 2026-03-24 Changed Package renamed — com.wayngamesassets.mgm.charactercontroller → wayn.games.mgm.character-controller Namespaces renamed — WAYN.Games.MGM.Locomotion.* → WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.* Assembly definitions renamed — All assemblies now use WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.* naming Editor menu path — WAYN Games/Locomotion → WAYN Games/MGM Character Controller Source generator project renamed — Wayn.Games.Locomotion.SourceGen → WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.SourceGen Added Upgrade guide — Migration instructions for 0.4.x → 0.5.0 [0.4.3] - 2026-03-24 Fixed Documentation build — Added v0.4.2 built docs and updated version redirect [0.4.2] - 2026-03-24 Changed Documentation structure — Migrated to new DocTools layout Removed Legacy documentation files — Removed old shell scripts, .meta files, and deprecated doc structure [0.4.1] - 2026-03-23 Added i18n documentation — French and Chinese translations for all manual and API pages Fixed Documentation navigation — Fixed broken relative links in navbar (API, LICENSE, CHANGELOG) API documentation — Validated and corrected v0.4.0 API docs against codebase [0.4.0] - 2026-03-22 Added Documentation site — Manual pages and DocFX deployment workflow for hosted docs XML documentation — Added and updated XML doc comments across the codebase Changed Source generator rewrite — Rewritten source generator with minimal boilerplate; ILocomotionStateMachineDefinition replaced by attribute-based discovery DIM lifecycle defaults — Source generator now emits explicit struct forwarding methods for default interface method lifecycle hooks Editor reorganized — Creation menus, script templates, and helpers moved into Editor/CreationMenus/ subfolder Locomotion script scaffolding — New CreateLocomotionScript menu and LocomotionScriptCreationMenus for streamlined script creation Package metadata — Updated display name, description, dependencies, and documentation URLs in package.json Removed Build artifacts from source control — Stopped tracking SrcGenProj~ build outputs [0.3.0] - 2026-03-18 Added Debug visualization system — Configurable debug display for contact points, movement paths, and collision impulses with per-entity color coding Move() method — Unified movement API with integrated debug visualization Configurable CollisionTolerance — Exposed collision tolerance and BodyType.Character detection on CharacterPhysicsProfile; added ContactType.Step Auto-generated assembly definitions — Editor scaffolding now generates runtime, authoring, and editor asmdef files with proper namespaces and GUID references ILocomotionSystem interface template — New DIM-based locomotion system interface generated by editor scaffolding, replacing inline boilerplate TestCharacter test state machine — Full test state machine under Tests/TestCharacter/ with runtime, authoring, and editor assemblies Changed ILocomotionState uses instance-method DIM pattern — State lifecycle methods are now instance methods with default interface implementations instead of static methods CharacterPhysicsProfile moved to ILocomotionContext — Decoupled from ILocomotionProfile so profiles no longer need to carry physics data Base authoring classes renamed — BaseCharacterAuthoring → BaseCharacterLocomotorAuthoring, BaseCharacterBaker → BaseCharacterLocomotorBaker Platform velocity inheritance — Replaced path-tracking system with MotionVelocity-based approach via ComputeInheritedDisplacement Post-collision impulse storage — Replaced NativeQueue<PostCollisionImpulse> with NativeParallelMultiHashMap for better parallel write performance Debug config inspector — Converted to UI Toolkit Locomotion system template simplified — Uses ILocomotionSystem DIM pattern; system struct now delegates to shared interface methods Generated file naming — All scaffolded files now prefixed with state machine name to avoid collisions across multiple state machines Test runtime namespace — Moved to WAYN.Games.MGM.Locomotion.Tests.Runtime Fixed Overlap resolution — Burst-compiled resolution for extreme overlap cases and low frame rate scenarios Dynamic body impulse scaling — Corrected impulse magnitude and platform velocity transfer Platform overlap and movement — Fixed characters clipping through or getting stuck on moving platforms Contact system and execute loop ordering — Overhauled contact detection; EvaluateTransition now runs once at the end of the loop Balance behavior — Fixed edge-balancing state detection Removed GenerateLocomotionSystem — Removed dead source generator method (superseded by DIM-based template) PhysicsProfileValue on ILocomotionProfile — Moved to context; profiles no longer carry physics configuration [0.2.0] - 2025-10-05 Added Support for custom state machine - Refactored systems to enable user defined state machine Source Generator - The package now relies on source generator to create the input and state machine execution systems. State Machine Creation Menu - Added a creation menu to set up a new state machine [0.1.0] - 2025-07-02 Added Modular locomotion architecture – Introduces a clean separation of concerns by splitting player input, character physics, and the visual model into distinct entities. This design supports both player-controlled and AI-controlled characters, and scales to multiple characters or players in local multiplayer scenarios. State-driven movement system – Character locomotion is governed by a flexible state machine with distinct states (e.g. idle, grounded, jumping, falling, sliding) and smooth automatic transitions between them. Movement behavior is defined by data-driven profiles, making it easy to tune speeds and jump parameters without code changes. Physics-based character controller – Uses physics under the hood for robust movement and collisions. Characters utilize a capsule collider and can step over small obstacles up to a defined height, as well as traverse slopes up to a maximum angle before sliding down steeper inclines. The system handles gravity and falling, inherits movement from moving platforms (so characters ride platforms smoothly), and includes solid collision resolution to prevent getting stuck on edges or corners. Integrated input handling – Built-in support for player input, character movement direction is calculated relative to the camera’s orientation, and an optional “aim” input allows the character to align with the camera direction for intuitive control. Input capture uses the new input system for easily supporting multiple control devices. Camera tracking for characters – A camera target tracking system automatically follows player characters to keep them in view. Integration with the new input system and PlayerInputManager allows natural support for split screen local multiplayer.The camera target follows smoothly the position and rotation in both single-player and local multiplayer scenarios. The solution supports use of Cinemachine cameras. Animation-ready character models – Visual presentation is decoupled from logic by spawning a separate model GameObject for rendering and animation. The locomotion system keeps the model’s transform in sync with the character’s state, enabling developers to hook up animations (e.g. running, jumping) to the character’s movement without affecting the underlying physics simulation. Customizable locomotion profiles – Character movement characteristics are driven by scriptable Locomotion Profile assets. These profiles define parameters like movement speed, acceleration, turning rate, allowed jump count, jump heights, and gravity settings for each character. This allows easy tweaking of locomotion behavior and per-character tuning by adjusting profile values in the editor. Example scenes – Included sample scenes demonstrate the locomotion system in action. A single-player example and a local multiplayer example are provided, showcasing basic player movement, jumping, camera follow, and multiple characters controlled simultaneously.

api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.A.title: Class BaseCharacterLocomotorAuthoring<TProfile>.BaseCharacterLocomotorBaker<TAuthoring, TStateMachine, TStates, TContext, TInput, TStateData> | MGM Character Controller
api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.A.summary: Class BaseCharacterLocomotorAuthoring<TProfile>.BaseCharacterLocomotorBaker<TAuthoring, TStateMachine, TStates, TContext, TInput, TStateData> Baker for the CharacterAuthoring. Converts the authoring data into one or more ECS entities with the following setup: Creates the primary character entity (manual‐override transform) and, if Playable is checked, adds a PlayableCharacter tag. If a ModelPrefab is assigned, spawns a separate “model” entity with: – PresentationGo (holds the prefab for rendering) – ControllingEntity (points back to the character entity) Applies the authoring transform to the character via LocalTransform. If a CharacterProfile is provided: – Generates or reuses a BlobAssetReference from the profile asset (and calls DependsOn so changes re-bake automatically) – Adds LocomotionInput to drive character movement from player input or AI Agent – Builds a CapsuleCollider from profile settings (CapsuleGeometry + CollisionFilter) and adds it as a PhysicsCollider blob asset – Registers the character with the physics world (PhysicsWorldIndex) – Adds LocomotionProfileReference (points to the blob asset), LocomotionData (initial velocity, jump count, etc.), LocomotionGravity (gravity multiplier) – Initializes the CharacterLocomotionStateMachine in the Falling state Inheritance object BaseCharacterLocomotorAuthoring<TProfile>.BaseCharacterLocomotorBaker<TAuthoring, TStateMachine, TStates, TContext, TInput, TStateData> FlyCamCharacterLocomotionAuthoring.FlyCamCharacterLocomotionAuthoringBaker LocomotionCharacterLocomotorLocomotionAuthoring.LocomotionCharacterLocomotorLocomotionAuthoringBaker TestCharacterCharacterLocomotionAuthoring.TestCharacterCharacterLocomotionAuthoringBaker Namespace: TContext, TInput, TStateData> : Baker<TAuthoring> where TAuthoring : BaseCharacterLocomotorAuthoring<TProfile> where TStateMachine : unmanaged, ILocomotionStateMachine<TStateMachine, TStates, TContext, TProfile, TInput, TStateData>, IComponentData where TStates : unmanaged where TContext : unmanaged, ILocomotionContext<TProfile, TInput, TStateData> where TInput : unmanaged, IComponentData where TStateData : unmanaged, IComponentData Type Parameters Name Description TAuthoring TStateMachine TStates TContext TInput TStateData Methods Bake(TAuthoring) Declaration public override sealed void Bake(TAuthoring authoring) Parameters Type Name Description TAuthoring authoring ContinueBaking() Allows for additional logic or processing during the baking process of a character. Derived classes can override this method to implement custom behavior for extending the baking process. This method is invoked after the BaseCharacterBaker.Bake(TAuthoring authoring) method. The authoring component and different baking entities can be accessed through the protected fields of the baker. Declaration protected virtual void ContinueBaking()

api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.A.title: Class BaseCharacterLocomotorAuthoring<TProfile> | MGM Character Controller
api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.A.summary: Class BaseCharacterLocomotorAuthoring<TProfile> Serves as a base class for authoring character entities with locomotion capabilities. Designed to work with a profile of type TProfile, enabling customization of character movement and behavior through specific profile definitions. Contains shared properties and functionality for setting up entities related to character locomotion, including rendering models and defining state machines through further specialization. The class includes a nested abstract baker class used for converting authoring data into ECS entities with the following configurations: Associates the authored character with a profile asset (defining locomotion behavior and rules). Supports optional assignment of a prefab model entity for visual representation. Facilitates the integration of locomotion state machines, states, and custom contexts specific to the movement profile. Inheritance object LocomotorAuthoring BaseCharacterLocomotorAuthoring<TProfile> FlyCamCharacterLocomotionAuthoring LocomotionCharacterLocomotorLocomotionAuthoring TestCharacterCharacterLocomotionAuthoring Namespace: WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Authoring Assembly: .dll Syntax public abstract class BaseCharacterLocomotorAuthoring<TProfile> : LocomotorAuthoring where TProfile : unmanaged, ILocomotionProfile Type Parameters Name Description TProfile The locomotion profile type derived from ILocomotionProfile. This profile encapsulates movement rules and parameters for the character. Fields PhysicsProfile Physics configuration (capsule dimensions, mass, slope limits) baked into a blob asset on the character entity. Declaration protected CharacterPhysicsProfile PhysicsProfile Field Value Type Description CharacterPhysicsProfile ProfileAsset The scriptable object that defines the locomotion profile for the character, specifying movement capabilities and behaviors in the game world. Declaration protected LocomotionProfileAsset<TProfile> ProfileAsset Field Value Type Description LocomotionProfileAsset<TProfile>

This structure is utilized to transfer character entity references during authoring. Implements IBufferElementData Inherited Members ValueType.Equals(object) ValueType.GetHashCode() ValueType.ToString() object.Equals(object, object) object.GetType() object.ReferenceEquals(object, object) Namespace: WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Authoring Assembly: .dll Syntax public struct ControlledCharactersBakingList : IBufferElementData Remarks ControlledCharactersBakingList references the root character entity while the ControlledCharacters used at runtime requires the Locomotion and Model which aren't available from the Baker. Therefore, we need to rely on a temporary baking component to reference the root character entity and retrieve its sub entities in the BasePlayerCharacterBakingSystem. api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.A.summary: Class PlayerInputAsset<TInput> The PlayerInputAsset is an abstract base class for defining player input behavior in a characters locomotion system. This class should be inherited to implement specific player input handling, such as mapping game inputs to a given input type. At runtime the asset is instantiated per player to allow caching of player specific reference (camera, etc...) in the asset instance. Remarks This asset reads the player input device but doesn't affect the controlled character input. To apply the player input to a character you need to implement the ICharacterLocomotionInput on your TInput Methods InitInputAsset(PlayerGameObjectInstance) Performs the initialization of the input asset, enabling necessary input actions, caching camera or other necessary information to process the player input. api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.A.title: Namespace WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Authoring | MGM Character Controller
api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.A.summary: Namespace WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Authoring Classes BaseCharacterLocomotorAuthoring<TProfile> Serves as a base class for authoring character entities with locomotion capabilities. Designed to work with a profile of type TProfile, enabling customization of character movement and behavior through specific profile definitions. Contains shared properties and functionality for setting up entities related to character locomotion, including rendering models and defining state machines through further specialization. The class includes a nested abstract baker class used for converting authoring data into ECS entities with the following configurations: Associates the authored character with a profile asset (defining locomotion behavior and rules). Supports optional assignment of a prefab model entity for visual representation. Facilitates the integration of locomotion state machines, states, and custom contexts specific to the movement profile. BaseCharacterLocomotorAuthoring<TProfile>.BaseCharacterLocomotorBaker<TAuthoring, TStateMachine, TStates, TContext, TInput, TStateData> Baker for the CharacterAuthoring. Converts the authoring data into one or more ECS entities with the following setup: Creates the primary character entity (manual‐override transform) and, if Playable is checked, adds a PlayableCharacter tag. If a ModelPrefab is assigned, spawns a separate “model” entity with: – PresentationGo (holds the prefab for rendering) – ControllingEntity (points back to the character entity) Applies the authoring transform to the character via LocalTransform. If a CharacterProfile is provided: – Generates or reuses a BlobAssetReference from the profile asset (and calls DependsOn so changes re-bake automatically) – Adds LocomotionInput to drive character movement from player input or AI Agent – Builds a CapsuleCollider from profile settings (CapsuleGeometry + CollisionFilter) and adds it as a PhysicsCollider blob asset – Registers the character with the physics world (PhysicsWorldIndex) – Adds LocomotionProfileReference (points to the blob asset), LocomotionData (initial velocity, jump count, etc.), LocomotionGravity (gravity multiplier) – Initializes the CharacterLocomotionStateMachine in the Falling state BasePlayerCharacterAuthoring<TInput> Abstract base class for authoring player character components. Provides support for player input assets and control over multiple characters. BasePlayerCharacterAuthoring<TInput>.BasePlayerCharacterBaker<TAuthoring> Baker class to convert PlayerAuthoring into ECS entity. CharacterFollowerAuthoring MonoBehaviour authoring component that marks a GameObject as a character follower entity. CharacterFollowerAuthoring.CharacterFollowerAuthoringBaker Baker that converts CharacterFollowerAuthoring into a CharacterFollower ECS component. CompositeEntityAuthoring Authoring component that marks a prefab root as a composite entity (Locomotor + Model hierarchy). Requires a LinkedEntityGroupAuthoring to be present on the same GameObject. CompositeEntityAuthoring.CompositeEntityAuthoringBaker Baker that converts CompositeEntityAuthoring to its ECS representation with manual transform override. LocomotionDebugConfigAuthoring Authoring component that adds per-character locomotion debug visualisation configuration. Attach to a character prefab alongside the locomotor authoring — fully optional. LocomotionProfileAsset<TProfile> Abstract ScriptableObject base class for locomotion profile assets. Concrete implementations populate the TProfile blob with authoring data during the baking process. LocomotorAuthoring Abstract base MonoBehaviour for all locomotor authoring components, used as a type-erased reference from follower authoring. MovingPlatformAuthoring The PlatformAuthoring class is responsible for configuring platform components and their associated properties within the Unity Editor. It is used to initialize platform behavior by baking it into ECS-compatible components, such as Rotor and Track. PlatformAuthoring Authoring component that marks a GameObject as a platform entity. Required by MovingPlatformAuthoring. PlayerGoAuthoring Most of the player components (camera, input, ...) still required MonoBehaviour. This authoring component has to be set on a game object prefab that will represent the game object side of the player and handle all MonoBehaviour interactions. PlayerInputAsset<TInput> The PlayerInputAsset is an abstract base class for defining player input behavior in a characters locomotion system. This class should be inherited to implement specific player input handling, such as mapping game inputs to a given input type. At runtime the asset is instantiated per player to allow caching of player specific reference (camera, etc...) in the asset instance. Structs BasePlayerCharacterBakingSystem A Unity ECS system that operates under the baking system filter. This system is part of the Locomotion.Authoring namespace and may contain logic related to the backing or processing of player character data during runtime. ControlledCharactersBakingList Temporary buffer element used during the baking process to store a list of entities representing controlled characters. This structure is utilized to transfer character entity references during authoring. Interfaces ICharacterLocomotionInput<TInput> Interface for character locomotion input components. Implement this on the TInput struct to allow accumulated input (e.g. jump triggers) collected each Update to be applied to the character during the fixed update step. ILocomotionInputReader<TInput> DIM interface for customizing how player input is read and initialized. The generated PlayerInputAsset implements this and calls the methods through interface dispatch. User overrides by implementing on their partial class. ILocomotionProfileBuilder<TProfile> DIM interface for customizing how a locomotion profile blob asset is populated. The generated ProfileAsset implements this and calls PopulateProfile through interface dispatch. User overrides by implementing the method on their partial class. Enums FollowStrategy Determines how the camera target follows the controlled character(s) each frame.

api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.R.title: Namespace WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Runtime.Components | MGM Character Controller
api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.R.summary: Namespace WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Runtime.Components Structs CharacterFollower Registers an entity to follow a CharacterPath. CharacterPhysicsProfile Represents a physics configuration profile for a character, encapsulating core properties that define collision behavior, dimensions, and interaction with physics layers. CharacterPhysicsProfileBlob ECS component wrapping a blob asset reference to a baked CharacterPhysicsProfile. ControlledCharacters List of character entity controlled by the player or AI Agent Impact Describes a collision impact event between two physics bodies. LocomotionContact Last ground or obstacle contact for a Locomotor entity. LocomotionGravity Encapsulates gravity settings for the character. This value can be updated by a system to simulate any arbitrary gravity direction. LocomotionInputAsset<TInput> Stores a reference to the original PlayerInputAsset used by a player. LocomotionInputInstance<TInput> Stores a reference to the PlayerInputAsset<TInput> instance of a player. LocomotionProfileBlob<TProfile> Locomotion profile in the form of a blob asset. This profile contains configuration and parameters necessary for defining locomotion behavior within a state machine. LocomotionVelocity Locomotion velocity for a character locomotor. Motion PhysicsBody Lightweight reference to a physics rigid body, pairing its index and entity. PlatformMover Component driving a platform entity along a predefined track at a given speed. PlayerCameraHandle Component storing a reference to the player camera's target transform. PlayerGameObjectInstance Set of references to mono objects. These are used to update and managed game object behaviors of the player entity from systems allowing for greater control on the player loop. PlayerGameObjectPrefab Set of references to mono objects. These are used to update and managed game object behaviors of the player entity from systems allowing for greater control on the player loop. PostCollisionImpulse Represents the impulse applied to a rigid body following a collision event. Rotor Component marking an entity as a rotating platform, providing its rotation axis. Track Buffer element defining one waypoint position on a platform track. Enums BodyType Classifies the physics body type of a collision contact surface. ContactType Classifies the geometry of the collision contact surface. InterpolationMode Controls whether the character's transform is updated via physics graphical smoothing interpolation.

api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.R.title: Struct ClosestColliderCastHitCollectorIgnoreSelf | MGM Character Controller
api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.R.summary: Struct ClosestColliderCastHitCollectorIgnoreSelf Struct for collecting the closest collider cast hits while ignoring a specific entity. Implements the ICollector interface to gather and manage collider cast hit results. Filters out results that do not meet defined criteria such as hits associated with an ignored entity or backface collisions. This is particularly useful in physics-related computations where certain entities need to be excluded, such as in character or object traversal and detection systems. Declaration public ClosestColliderCastHitCollectorIgnoreSelf(Entity ignoredEntity, PhysicsWorld physicsWorld, float maxDistance, float3 castDirection, bool ignoreDynamicBodies = false) Parameters Type Name Description Entity ignoredEntity Entity to exclude from results (typically the casting character). PhysicsWorld physicsWorld Physics world used for dynamic-body and back-face checks. float maxDistance Initial maximum hit fraction; tightens as closer hits are found. float3 castDirection Direction of the cast, used to reject hits facing away from the cast. bool ignoreDynamicBodies When true, dynamic bodies are also excluded from results. Move() Projects the current displacements against the active contact surface, then advances the character to the next obstacle via collide-and-slide. Ground contacts cancel vertical displacement and project horizontal displacement along the slope. Walls attempt step detection before sliding. Ceilings project both displacements away from the surface normal. Works for any dynamic body (kinematic or fully-simulated), not just entities with a DynamicBodyPath buffer. Because Unity.Physics damps velocities after integrating position each frame, the stored Unity.Physics.MotionVelocity is the post-damped value; this method reverses the damping factor to recover the velocity that actually drove the platform's displacement during the previous frame. api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.R.title: Enum OverlapState | MGM Character Controller
api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.R.summary: Enum OverlapState Describes how the character's collider overlaps with surrounding geometry after collision resolution. Namespace: WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Runtime.StateMachine Assembly: .dll Syntax public enum OverlapState Fields Name Description Crush The character is fully enclosed by surrounding geometry with no escape direction. None No overlap detected; the character is free to move. Pinch The character is caught between two converging surfaces. Push The character is overlapping a single surface and being pushed out.

api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.R.title: Namespace WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Runtime.StateMachine | MGM Character Controller
api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.R.summary: Namespace WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Runtime.StateMachine Classes EvaluateAfterAttribute Specifies that this transition should be evaluated after the specified transition type within the same source state. Follows the same pattern as Unity's [UpdateBefore]/[UpdateAfter] system ordering. EvaluateBeforeAttribute Specifies that this transition should be evaluated before the specified transition type within the same source state. Follows the same pattern as Unity's [UpdateBefore]/[UpdateAfter] system ordering. LocomotionGenerationOptionsAttribute Optional attribute on the LocomotionStateMachine anchor struct to control source generation behavior. LocomotionStateAttribute Marks a struct as a locomotion state belonging to a specific state machine. The generator auto-generates the enum, adds the concrete I{Name}State interface via partial extension, and wires up the state machine dispatch. LocomotionStateMachineAttribute Marks a struct as a locomotion state machine anchor. The struct's name determines all generated type names by convention: XyzStateMachine → prefix Xyz → XyzProfile, XyzInput, XyzStateData, XyzContext, XyzStates (enum). LocomotionTransitionAttribute Marks a struct as a locomotion transition. The fromState and toState parameters specify the source and target states. Structs BaseLocomotionContext Base context available to all locomotion state machine. Contains the minimal core state data (Velocity, Position, ...) and information (Entity, Collider, PhysicsWorld,...) required for locomotion state logic and platform handling logic. LocomotionInputDispatcher Static dispatcher that invokes ApplyInputsToCharacter(in TInput) through a constrained generic call so Burst devirtualises the DIM at compile time. LocomotionJob<TStateMachine, TStates, TContext, TProfile, TInput, TStateData> Burst-compiled ECS chunk job that drives the full locomotion execute loop for every Locomotor entity in a chunk: inherit displacement, solve overlaps, handle impulses, integrate, collide-and-slide, snap-to-ground, and evaluate transitions. LocomotionStateBehaviorDispatcher Static dispatcher that invokes ILocomotionState<TStates, TContext, TProfile, TInput, TStateData> lifecycle methods on a zero-size default instance of TState. Burst devirtualises each call at compile time — no boxing occurs. Interfaces ILocomotionContext<TProfile, TInput, TStateData> Interface enforcing the minimal information need for the context of locomotion state machine. It enforces the encapsulation configuration, input, and state data. ILocomotionProfile Marker interface for locomotion profile data. Implement this interface on an unmanaged struct that holds per-profile configuration values (e.g. movement speed, jump height) baked into a blob asset and supplied to the locomotion job. ILocomotionStateMachine<TStateMachine, TStates, TContext, TProfile, TInput, TStateData> Interface that defines the structure of a character locomotion state machine, responsible for managing state transitions, physics integration, collision resolution, and other locomotion behaviors of characters. ILocomotionState<TStates, TContext, TProfile, TInput, TStateData> Interface for defining locomotion states within a character movement state machine. ILocomotionTransition<TContext, TProfile, TInput, TStateData> Base interface for locomotion transitions. A transition evaluates whether the state machine should move between two states specified via the LocomotionTransitionAttribute. Enums LocomotionGenerationFlags Flags for controlling locomotion source generation behavior. OverlapState Describes how the character's collider overlaps with surrounding geometry after collision resolution.

api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.R.title: Namespace WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Runtime.Systems | MGM Character Controller
api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.R.summary: Namespace WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Runtime.Systems Structs ApplyPostCollisionImpulseJob Burst-compiled job that processes each pending post-collision impulse and applies the appropriate velocity change to the affected physics bodies. CameraRigFollowControlledCharacterSystem A system responsible for managing the camera rig to follow the currently controlled character in a game scene. CameraRigFollowControlledCharacterSystem.Singleton Singleton component storing the camera transform array and the corresponding player entity list. CharacterContactSystem ECS system that runs during the physics simulation group to intercept contact events between character colliders and other physics bodies, classify body types, and accumulate de-collision and velocity impulse data for later processing by the locomotion job. CharacterContactSystem.CharacterContactClearMapJob Burst-compiled job that clears the de-collision and velocity impulse maps at the start of each frame. CharacterContactSystem.CharacterContactJob Burst-compiled contacts job that classifies each contact body, disables the default physics jacobian, and records de-collision offsets and velocity impulses for character entities. CharacterContactSystem.ExternalImpulse Represents a velocity impulse applied to a character as a result of a physics contact. CharacterContactSystem.Singleton Singleton holding the per-frame contact data maps produced by CharacterContactSystem. CharacterFollowerSystem A system responsible for updating the transformations of entities that follow a character within the game world. CharacterFollowerSystem.CharacterFollowerJob Burst-compiled parallel job that copies the followed entity's LocalToWorld to each follower. ILocomotionSystem<TStateMachine, TStates, TContext, TProfile, TInput, TStateData>.LocomotionJobData Aggregated data bag holding all component type handles and lookups required by the locomotion job. LocomotionSystemBehaviorDispatcher Static dispatcher that invokes ILocomotionSystem<TStateMachine, TStates, TContext, TProfile, TInput, TStateData> DIM lifecycle methods on a zero-size default instance of TSystem. Mirrors LocomotionStateBehaviorDispatcher — uses default(TSystem).Method(ref data) for zero-boxing DIM dispatch via constrained callvirt. PlatformMovementJob Burst-compiled parallel job that moves platform entities along their configured track waypoints. PlatformMovementSystem This system updates the position and rotation of platforms. PlatformRotateJob Burst-compiled parallel job that rotates platform entities around their configured axis each frame. PlayerGoManagerSystem Manages the lifecycle of the player game object. It ensures it's instantiation from the prefab for every player. When a player entity gets destroyed, the cleanup component ensures the destruction of the camera rig game object. PlayerGoManagerSystem.CleanUpControlledPlayerCharacterJob Parallel job that removes destroyed character entities from each player's ControlledCharacters buffer. PostCollisionImpulseComparer Comparer that sorts post-collision impulses by impactee then impactor entity index for deterministic application. PostCollisionImpulseSystem ECS system that dequeues and applies post-collision impulses accumulated during the locomotion job. Runs after the physics group to ensure all collision responses have been resolved. PostCollisionImpulseSystem.Singleton Singleton component holding the shared post-collision impulse queue. Interfaces ILocomotionSystem<TStateMachine, TStates, TContext, TProfile, TInput, TStateData> Interface implemented by generated locomotion systems. Provides the contract for scheduling and running the LocomotionJob<TStateMachine, TStates, TContext, TProfile, TInput, TStateData> each fixed update step.

api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.S.summary: Class LocomotionPlayerInputAsset A specialized ScriptableObject representing a player's input asset for basic locomotion. This class provides the mechanisms to map inputs from a player (such as key presses or joystick motions) into meaningful locomotion values for gameplay behavior. It uses the camera's perspective to determine movement vector directions and handles actions like jumping. Inheritance object PlayerInputAsset<LocomotionInput> LocomotionPlayerInputAsset Implements IComponentData Namespace: WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Samples.Locomotion.Runtime Assembly: .dll Syntax [Serializable] public class LocomotionPlayerInputAsset : PlayerInputAsset<LocomotionInput>, IComponentData Fields JumpAction Input action used to detect jump button presses. Declaration public InputActionReference JumpAction Field Value Type Description InputActionReference MovementAction Input action used to read the 2-axis movement vector (e.g., WASD or left stick). Declaration public InputActionReference MovementAction Field Value Type Description InputActionReference Methods InitInputAsset(PlayerGameObjectInstance) Initialises the input asset by caching the camera and UnityEngine.InputSystem.PlayerInput components from the player game object, and configures Cinemachine cameras for this player. Declaration public override void InitInputAsset(PlayerGameObjectInstance playerGameObjectInstance) Parameters Type Name Description PlayerGameObjectInstance playerGameObjectInstance The instantiated player game object providing camera and input references. Overrides PlayerInputAsset<LocomotionInput>.InitInputAsset(PlayerGameObjectInstance) ReadInputsFromAsset(out LocomotionInput) Reads movement and jump inputs from the player's input actions and converts them to world-space values relative to the camera's orientation. Declaration public override void ReadInputsFromAsset(out LocomotionInput inputs) Parameters Type Name Description LocomotionInput inputs The populated LocomotionInput struct output. Overrides PlayerInputAsset<LocomotionInput>.ReadInputsFromAsset(out LocomotionInput) Implements IComponentData

api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.S.summary: Namespace WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Samples.Locomotion.Runtime Classes LocomotionPlayerInputAsset A specialized ScriptableObject representing a player's input asset for basic locomotion. This class provides the mechanisms to map inputs from a player (such as key presses or joystick motions) into meaningful locomotion values for gameplay behavior. It uses the camera's perspective to determine movement vector directions and handles actions like jumping. LocomotionProfileAsset A scriptable object to define the configuration for a basic character profile. api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.T.title: Enum SpawnPattern | MGM Character Controller
api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.T.summary: Enum SpawnPattern Identifies which spatial layout is used when batch-spawning entities. Namespace: WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Tests.Runtime Assembly: .dll Syntax public enum SpawnPattern Fields Name Description Circle Evenly-spaced ring on the XZ plane. Cube 3-D axis-aligned cube of entities. Grid Flat 2-D grid on the XZ plane. Line Single straight line of entities. RandomScatter Uniform random scatter inside an axis-aligned box. SingleAtClickPosition Raycast from the mouse cursor; spawn one entity at the hit point. Sphere Golden-spiral distribution across a sphere surface.

api/WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.T.summary: Struct SpawningSystem ECS system that spawns prefab entities in configurable spatial patterns in response to player input. Implements ISystem Inherited Members ValueType.Equals(object) ValueType.GetHashCode() ValueType.ToString() object.Equals(object, object) object.GetType() object.ReferenceEquals(object, object) Namespace: WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Tests.Runtime Assembly: .dll Syntax public struct SpawningSystem : ISystem Remarks Key bindings: Tab — cycle to the next SpawnPattern. Enter — batch-spawn with the active pattern. Shift+LMB — spawn a single entity (raycast hit when pattern is SingleAtClickPosition). RMB — log current entity count. Backspace — destroy all entities with PhysicsVelocity. If no SpawnConfigData singleton is present the system falls back to sphere defaults. index.html.title: MGM Character Controller | MGM Character Controller
index.html.summary: MGM Character Controller MGM Character Controller is a modular, high-performance character locomotion package for Unity 6, built entirely on the DOTS stack (Entities, Unity.Physics, Burst, Jobs). It provides a state-machine-driven approach to character movement with full source generation, making it both easy to extend and blazingly fast at runtime. When to Use This Package Use MGM Character Controller when you need: A kinematic character controller built on Unity ECS State-machine-driven movement with clear per-state lifecycle hooks Burst-compiled parallel locomotion for many characters Clean separation between physics simulation (ECS) and presentation (GameObjects) This package is not for projects that use MonoBehaviour-based character controllers, do not use Unity DOTS, or need a drop-in replacement for Unity's built-in CharacterController component. Key Features Pure ECS locomotion with Burst-compiled jobs for maximum throughput State-machine-driven movement with a clear lifecycle per state Source-generated boilerplate via Roslyn analyzers, so you focus on gameplay logic Collide-and-slide physics with automatic step detection, slope handling, and ground snapping Moving platform support with velocity inheritance and rotation handling Hybrid GameObject layer for seamless integration with animations, audio, and cameras Built-in debug visualization for contact points, movement paths, and collision impulses Editor scaffolding tools that generate the entire state machine folder structure in one click Limitations Characters are kinematic only - no ragdoll or force-driven movement out of the box Profile changes require re-baking (exit and re-enter Play mode) Debug visualization is editor-only and adds overhead when enabled No built-in animation state machine bridge (you connect your Animator on the model GameObject) Quick Links Installation - Install the package and verify it works Quickstart - Import the sample and see a character running in minutes Getting Started - Create your first character from scratch Architecture Overview - How the pieces fit together The Execution Loop - What happens each fixed update Creating a State Machine - Step-by-step guide Writing Custom States - The state lifecycle in depth Samples - Sample index with study order Troubleshooting - Common issues and fixes API Reference - Full API documentation Requirements Requirement Version Unity 6000.3+ Entities 1.3.9 Unity.Physics 1.3.8 Burst 1.8.18 Collections 2.5.4 Mathematics 1.3.2 Package Info Package name wayn.games.mgm.character-controller Version 0.5.0 Author WAYN Games License See LICENSE.md Next Step If you already have the package installed, go to the Quickstart. Otherwise, start with Installation.

manual/architecture.html.title: Architecture Overview | MGM Character Controller
manual/architecture.html.summary:
Architecture Overview This page explains the high-level design of the MGM Character Controller: the entity types, the role of each layer, and how data flows through the system. Design Philosophy The package is built around three principles: Performance first - All locomotion logic runs in Burst-compiled parallel jobs. No managed allocations on the hot path. Separation of concerns - Physics simulation (ECS) is decoupled from presentation (GameObjects). You can run thousands of characters without any GameObjects at all. Extensibility through composition - Instead of a monolithic character controller, you compose behavior from a state machine, a physics profile, an input component, and a context, all of which are user-defined types. Entity Types The package defines three distinct entity roles. Understanding them is essential. Locomotor (Pure ECS) The Locomotor is the primary character entity. It lives entirely in ECS and carries all the components needed for physics-based movement: Component Purpose LocalTransform World position and rotation PhysicsCollider Capsule collider for collision detection PhysicsVelocity Required by Unity.Physics to consider the character a non static entity. (always zeroed; the package drives movement directly) LocomotionVelocity The character's actual velocity, maintained by the state machine LocomotionGravity Gravity vector applied to this character LocomotionContact Current surface contact information (normal, type, entity) CharacterPhysicsProfileBlob Baked physics configuration (capsule size, mass, slopes) LocomotionProfileBlob Baked gameplay profile (speeds, jump force, etc.) TStateMachine Source-generated state machine component (current state + dispatch) TInput User-defined input component TStateData User-defined per-state persistent data The Locomotor is a kinematic body. Unlike Unity.Physics dynamic bodies, it does not respond to forces automatically. Instead, the locomotion job computes displacement each frame and moves the collider through the world using a collide-and-slide algorithm. Model (Hybrid ECS + GameObject) The Model is an optional entity that provides visual representation. It carries a reference to a GameObject prefab that gets instantiated at runtime. The model entity follows the Locomotor entity via the CharacterFollower component. Why separate them? Because: Locomotion logic runs in parallel Burst jobs that cannot touch GameObjects Visual updates (animation, particles, audio) need MonoBehaviour access You can run headless simulations (server, AI training) with zero GameObjects Player (Input Source) The Player entity represents an input source. It owns a ControlledCharacters buffer that lists which Locomotor entities it controls. A Player also has: A PlayerGameObjectPrefab referencing a GameObject with camera, input system, and UI A PlayerGameObjectInstance linking to the instantiated runtime copy An LocomotionInputAsset that reads hardware input and maps it to the input component Players are managed by the PlayerGoManagerSystem, which instantiates and destroys their GameObjects as players join and leave. Assembly Structure The package is split into three runtime assemblies: WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Runtime Core ECS logic (state machine, physics, components, systems) WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Hybrid GameObject bridge (transform sync, entity-GO mapping) WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Editor Editor tools (scaffolding, inspectors) Your game code references Runtime and optionally Hybrid. The Editor assembly is editor-only and provides scaffolding menus. Data Flow Here is how data flows through the system each fixed update: flowchart TD HW["Hardware Input"] PIA["PlayerInputAsset.ReadInputsFromAsset()
MonoBehaviour, Update rate"] ICI["ICharacterLocomotionInput.ApplyInputsToCharacter()
Accumulates triggers"] IRS["InputReaderSystem (generated)
Fixed update, writes TInput component"] LJ["LocomotionJob (Burst, parallel)
Fixed update, reads all components"] TR["Transform updated on Locomotor entity"] CFS["CharacterFollowerSystem
Syncs Model entity to Locomotor"] EGO["EntityGoTransformSyncSystem (Hybrid)
Copies entity transforms to GameObjects"] GO["GameObject renders on screen"] HW --> PIA --> ICI --> IRS --> LJ subgraph LocomotionJob S1["HandleExternalImpulses"] S2["Integrate"] S3["SolveOverlaps"] S4["CollideAndSlide (loop)"] S5["SnapToGround"] S6["InheritDisplacementFromMotionVelocity"] S7["EvaluateTransition"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 end LJ --> LocomotionJob --> TR --> CFS --> EGO --> GO Source Generation A Roslyn source generator reads your anchor struct annotated with [LocomotionStateMachine] and your state structs annotated with [LocomotionState] and produces: State machine component - An IComponentData struct that holds the current state and dispatches to the correct state implementation Locomotion system - An ISystem that schedules the LocomotionJob each fixed update Input reader system - An ISystem that reads input from the PlayerInputAsset and writes it to the TInput component Player input manager - Manages per-player input asset instances This means you never write scheduling code, component type handle boilerplate, or state dispatch switch statements. You define your states, annotate your structs, and the generator handles the rest. Blob Assets Configuration data (physics profiles, locomotion profiles, debug config) is stored as blob assets rather than regular components. Blob assets are: Immutable at runtime (safe for parallel access) Shared across entities with identical configuration (memory efficient) Created during baking from ScriptableObject authoring assets This means changing a profile value requires re-baking (exiting and re-entering play mode, or modifying the asset in the editor). Next Steps The Execution Loop - Detailed breakdown of each step in the locomotion job Creating a State Machine - How to define your own state machine Samples - Study the sample code to see this architecture in practice

manual/best-practices.html.title: Best Practices | MGM Character Controller
manual/best-practices.html.summary: Best Practices Opinionated recommendations for building with the MGM Character Controller. Project Structure One state machine per character archetype Create separate state machine types for characters with fundamentally different movement (e.g., ground characters vs vehicles vs flying). Do not try to fit all movement into a single state enum. Keep states small and focused Each state should handle one movement behavior. If a state has many branches ("if grounded do X, else if swimming do Y, else..."), split it into separate states with transitions between them. Use sub-profiles for each state Group profile parameters by the state that uses them: public partial struct MyProfile : ILocomotionProfile { public MovingProfile Moving; public FallingProfile Falling; public JumpingProfile Jumping; public DashProfile Dash; } This keeps the Inspector organized and makes it obvious which parameters affect which state. State Logic Always use ref access for BaseContext Declare a ref local at the top of your state methods to avoid copy-modify-writeback overhead: public void OnIntegrateState(ref MyContext ctx) { ref BaseLocomotionContext baseContext = ref ctx.BaseContext; // All modifications go through the ref } Exception: OnResolveStateCollisions requires copy-modify-writeback because Move() modifies the context internally. Accumulate triggers, overwrite continuous inputs In ApplyInputsToCharacter: Continuous (movement direction, aim): overwrite with the latest value Triggers (jump, dash, fire): accumulate with += Always consume triggers in your state logic by resetting them to zero after reading. Share computation logic, not state Use internal static helper methods to share physics computations across states (e.g., FallingState.ComputeGravityAcceleration). Do not share state data - each state should manage its own initialization and cleanup via OnEnterState / OnExitState. Do not add platform velocity manually The ComputeInheritedDisplacement step handles platform velocity automatically. Adding it in OnIntegrateState causes double-application. The only exception is OnEnterState for airborne states (e.g., jumping off a platform), where you add PlatformHorizontalVelocity to the character's own velocity to preserve momentum. Transitions Use the [LocomotionTransition] attribute pattern Declare transitions as small structs with [LocomotionTransition] and control priority with [EvaluateBefore] / [EvaluateAfter]: [LocomotionTransition(typeof(IdleState), typeof(JumpingState))] [EvaluateBefore(typeof(IdleToMoving))] public struct IdleToJumping : IMyTransition { public bool ShouldTransition(ref MyContext ctx) => ctx.BaseContext.IsGroundingContact && ctx.Input.JumpTrigger > 0; } This is clearer than inline EvaluateTransitions methods and lets the source generator handle dispatch. Put critical transitions first Order transitions so the most important ones evaluate first. Typical priority: jump > move > fall > slide. A [EvaluateBefore] on jump over move ensures pressing jump while running works. Performance Keep all state code Burst-compatible Avoid managed types (strings, classes, arrays) in state logic - states run inside Burst-compiled jobs No managed types (string, class, arrays) in state, context, profile, input, or state data structs Use shared colliders By default, capsule colliders are shared across entities with identical geometry and filter settings. Only add ForceUniqueColliderAuthoring if you need runtime shape modification per entity. Use shared profile blob assets All entities referencing the same profile asset share the same blob memory. Design your profiles so that common character types use the same asset. Only enable debug visualization when investigating Debug components add dynamic buffers and per-frame recording overhead. Remove LocomotionDebugConfigAuthoring from entities you are not actively debugging. Debugging Use debug visualization before adding logs The built-in debug visualization shows contact points, movement paths, and impulses per execution stage. This is faster than logging and works inside Burst jobs. Check transition priority when states misbehave If a character enters the wrong state, check [EvaluateBefore] ordering. A higher-priority transition may be firing before the one you expect. Verify contact types Many issues come from incorrect contact classification. Log or inspect ctx.BaseContext.LocomotionContact.ValueRO.ContactType to confirm whether the surface is Ground, Wall, or Ceiling. Team Workflow One profile asset per character class Use ScriptableObject profile assets so designers can tune parameters without touching code. Check these assets into version control. Name states and transitions descriptively Use the {Source}To{Target} naming convention for transitions (e.g., IdleToJumping, FallingToMoving). This makes the transition graph readable at a glance. Test states in isolation When adding a new state, start with just one transition into it and one transition out. Verify the core behavior works before wiring up all transitions. Next Steps Writing Custom States - State lifecycle reference Extending the Package - Advanced customization Troubleshooting - Common issues and fixes

manual/creating-state-machine.html.title: Creating a State Machine | MGM Character Controller
manual/creating-state-machine.html.summary: Creating a State Machine This guide explains how to create a complete locomotion state machine from scratch, either using the editor scaffolding tool or by hand. Using the Editor Tool (Recommended) The fastest way to create a state machine is through the Unity editor menu: Right-click in the Project window where you want the state machine folder Select Assets > Create > WAYN Games > MGM Character Controller > StateMachine Type a name for your state machine (e.g., Vehicle, Swimming, Flying) Press Enter The tool generates all required files with proper naming, namespaces, and wiring. You can immediately start customizing the generated code. Understanding What Gets Generated When you create a state machine named MyCharacter, the tool generates these types: The Anchor Struct [LocomotionStateMachine] public partial struct MyCharacterStateMachine { } This struct is the heart of the state machine. The [LocomotionStateMachine] attribute is parameterless - all type names are derived by convention from the struct name. For MyCharacterStateMachine, the prefix MyCharacter produces: MyCharacterContext, MyCharacterProfile, MyCharacterInput, MyCharacterStateData, and MyCharacterStates (the generated enum). States are separate structs annotated with [LocomotionState], linking them back to the state machine: [LocomotionState(typeof(MyCharacterStateMachine))] public partial struct IdleState { } The Context The context struct is source-generated from the anchor struct. You do not write it yourself. The generated struct looks like: // Source-generated - do not create manually public partial struct MyCharacterContext : ILocomotionContext<MyCharacterProfile, MyCharacterInput, MyCharacterStateData> { public BaseLocomotionContext BaseContext { get; set; } public CharacterPhysicsProfile CharacterPhysicsProfile { get; set; } public MyCharacterProfile Profile { get; set; } public MyCharacterInput Input { get; set; } public MyCharacterStateData StateData { get; set; } } The context is a data bag that is passed by reference through every state lifecycle method. It wraps: BaseLocomotionContext - The core physics state (position, velocity, displacement, contacts, gravity) CharacterPhysicsProfile - The baked capsule/physics configuration Your custom profile, input, and state data Because the context is partial, you can add custom properties and method overrides (like ComputeGroundSnappingDistance) in your own partial declaration. Tip If you frequently modify BaseContext fields, declare it as a public field in your partial declaration rather than using the auto-property. This lets you use ref access: ref BaseLocomotionContext baseContext = ref ctx.BaseContext; The Profile [Serializable] public partial struct MyCharacterProfile { // Add your configuration fields here: // public float MaxSpeed; // public float JumpForce; } The ILocomotionProfile interface is added automatically by the source generator via the partial mechanism. Profile data is baked into a blob asset during entity conversion. It is immutable at runtime and shared across all entities that reference the same profile asset. Put all tunable gameplay parameters here. The Input public partial struct MyCharacterInput { // Add your input fields here: // public float3 Movement; // public float JumpTrigger; public void ApplyInputsToCharacter(in MyCharacterInput playerInputs) { // Copy or accumulate input values here } } The ICharacterLocomotionInput<MyCharacterInput> and IComponentData interfaces are added automatically by the source generator via the partial mechanism. The input struct is an ECS component. It receives values from the PlayerInputAsset at the Update rate and accumulates them for the fixed update. The ApplyInputsToCharacter method is called every Update frame by the generated input reader system. Warning Trigger inputs (like jump) must be accumulated, not overwritten. If the Update rate is higher than the fixed update rate, a trigger press could be missed. Accumulate with JumpTrigger += playerInputs.JumpTrigger and consume it in your state logic by resetting it to zero. The StateData public partial struct MyCharacterStateData { // Add per-state persistent data here: // public int JumpCount; // public float StateTimer; } The IComponentData interface is added automatically by the source generator via the partial mechanism. State data persists across frames and state transitions. Unlike the context (which is rebuilt each frame from components), state data is stored as an ECS component and survives between frames. The Profile Asset [CreateAssetMenu(...)] public class MyCharacterProfileAsset : LocomotionProfileAsset<MyCharacterProfile> { // Serialized fields matching your profile struct public override ref MyCharacterProfile BuildBlobAsset(ref BlobBuilder blobBuilder) { ref MyCharacterProfile profile = ref blobBuilder.ConstructRoot<MyCharacterProfile>(); // Copy serialized fields to blob return ref profile; } } This ScriptableObject is what you assign in the Inspector. It serializes your profile data and builds the blob asset during baking. The Input Asset [CreateAssetMenu(...)] public class MyCharacterPlayerInputAsset : PlayerInputAsset<MyCharacterInput> { public override void ReadInputsFromAsset(out MyCharacterInput inputs) { // Read from Input System and populate inputs } public override void InitInputAsset(PlayerGameObjectInstance instance) { // Cache camera, PlayerInput component, etc. } } The input asset reads hardware input and converts it to your input struct. It is a ScriptableObject that gets instantiated per player at runtime, so each player can cache their own camera and input device references. The Authoring Components Two authoring MonoBehaviours are generated: Character Locomotor Authoring - Placed on the character entity in the sub-scene. References the profile asset and configures the physics profile. Player Character Authoring - Placed on the player entity. References the character to control and the input asset. The Locomotion System The locomotion system is fully source-generated. It implements ILocomotionSystem<TStateMachine, TStates, TContext, TProfile, TInput, TStateData> and ISystem, scheduling the LocomotionJob each fixed update. You never need to write this system yourself. To customize system behavior, override the DIM lifecycle methods (OnLocomotionSystemCreate, OnLocomotionSystemUpdate, OnLocomotionSystemDestroy) via a partial interface declaration. See Customizing the Locomotion System for details. What the Source Generator Produces In addition to the files you write, the Roslyn source generator produces (at compile time, invisible in the Project window): State machine component (MyCharacterStateMachine) - An IComponentData struct with a CurrentState field and dispatch methods that call the correct state implementation based on the enum value Input reader system - Reads from the PlayerInputAsset each Update frame and writes to the TInput component ISystem lifecycle methods - Generated OnCreate, OnUpdate, and OnDestroy on your locomotion system struct that schedule the LocomotionJob and manage type handles You never need to write or maintain this code. The generator reads your enum and produces it automatically. Adding a New State After the initial generation, use the editor menu to add states: Right-click in the Project window Select Assets > Create > WAYN Games > MGM Character Controller > State Name the state (e.g., Swimming) This generates a new state struct file annotated with [LocomotionState(typeof(MyCharacterStateMachine))]. The source generator automatically adds the state to the generated enum. Alternatively, create the state struct manually and annotate it with [LocomotionState(typeof(YourStateMachine))]. See Writing Custom States for the struct template. Manual Creation If you prefer to create everything by hand (or need to understand what the tools do), follow this checklist: Create an anchor partial struct with [LocomotionStateMachine] attribute (e.g., MyCharacterStateMachine) Create a partial struct for profile data (named {Prefix}Profile) Create a partial struct for input data with ApplyInputsToCharacter (named {Prefix}Input) Create a partial struct for state data (named {Prefix}StateData) Create at least one state partial struct with [LocomotionState(typeof(YourStateMachine))] Create a LocomotionProfileAsset<TProfile> ScriptableObject Create a PlayerInputAsset<TInput> ScriptableObject Register the generic input component: [assembly: RegisterGenericComponentType(typeof(LocomotionInputAsset<TInput>))] The source generator produces the context struct, state enum, locomotion system, authoring components, and all interface implementations from these inputs. The editor tool does all of this in one click, which is why it is recommended. Next Steps Writing Custom States - Implement state logic The Execution Loop - Understand when each method is called Recipe: Add a New Transition - Wire up transitions between states Samples - See a complete state machine implementation

manual/debug-visualization.html.title: Debug Visualization | MGM Character Controller
manual/debug-visualization.html.summary: Debug Visualization The MGM Character Controller includes a built-in debug visualization system that renders contact points, movement paths, and collision impulses directly in the Scene view. This is invaluable for diagnosing movement issues, tuning physics parameters, and understanding what the locomotion system is doing each frame. Enabling Debug Visualization Debug visualization is opt-in per entity: Select the character entity's authoring GameObject in the sub-scene Add a LocomotionDebugConfigAuthoring component Configure which visualizations you want to see Enter Play mode The debug config is baked into a blob asset, so changes require re-entering Play mode. Debug Data Types Path Points Path points show the character's position at each stage of the execution loop. They visualize the trajectory the character takes through a single frame, including each bounce of the collide-and-slide algorithm. Contact Points Contact points mark where the character's collider touches the environment. They are color-coded by contact type: Contact Type What It Means Ground The surface is walkable (slope below MaxSlope) Wall The surface is too steep to walk on Ceiling The surface is above the character None No contact this frame Impulse Points Impulse points show where collision forces are applied, both forces the character receives and forces it applies to other bodies. These help diagnose unexpected velocity changes or interactions with dynamic objects. Debug Stages Each visualization point is tagged with the execution stage where it was recorded: Stage Description Start Before any processing SolveOverlaps After overlap resolution CollideAndSlide After collide-and-slide SnapToGround After the ground snapping step InheritDisplacement After platform velocity computation This lets you pinpoint exactly which stage is producing unexpected behavior. Components and Systems LocomotionDebugConfig A blob asset storing debug configuration per entity. Controls visualization through duration floats (PathSeconds, ContactSeconds, ImpulseSeconds) - a duration of zero or less disables that category - and per-stage/per-type color and shape settings. LocomotionDebugRecorder A static utility that records debug points into dynamic buffers during job execution. Called from within the locomotion job when debug buffers are present. Debug Buffers Three IBufferElementData types store the recorded points: LocomotionDebugPathPoint - Position + stage + frame index LocomotionDebugContactPoint - Position + normal + contact type + body type + frame index LocomotionDebugImpulsePoint - Position + direction + magnitude + body type + is-applied flag + frame index LocomotionDebugDisplaySystem Reads the debug buffers and renders visualizations using the Unity.DebugDisplay API (Burst-compatible line and arrow drawing via PhysicsDebugDisplaySystem). LocomotionDebugFrameCounterSystem Maintains a global frame counter used to tag debug points. This allows the display system to distinguish between points from different frames and optionally show history. Performance Considerations Debug visualization adds overhead: Memory: Each entity with debug enabled allocates three dynamic buffers that grow each frame CPU: Recording points in the locomotion job adds branch checks and buffer writes Rendering: Drawing gizmos happens on the main thread For this reason: Only enable debug on entities you are actively investigating Disable debug visualization in builds The debug components are automatically excluded when the authoring component is not present Custom Debug Inspector The LocomotionDebugConfigAuthoringEditor provides a custom Inspector for the debug config authoring component. It shows a visual preview of the configured debug options including duration settings, colors, and contact shapes. Tips for Debugging Character falls through ground Enable path points and ground snap visualization. Check if: The snap-to-ground capsule cast is reaching the ground (path point should show a downward segment) The contact type at the end of the frame is Ground Character gets stuck on walls Enable contact points. Check if: The contact is classified as Wall vs Ground The MaxSlope threshold is correct Step detection is attempting and failing (check MaxStepHeight) Character launched unexpectedly Enable impulse points. Check if: External impulses are being applied (from CharacterContactSystem) A dynamic body collision is injecting unexpected velocity Platform inheritance is double-applied State transitions not firing This is a logic issue, not a physics issue. Add Debug.Log calls in your transition helper methods (temporarily, for non-Burst debugging) or check the state machine component in the Entity Inspector. Profiler Integration Beyond visual debugging, the package integrates with Unity's Profiler: ProfilerMarkerExtensions Burst-compiled profiler marker utilities that add custom markers to the Profiler timeline. These markers show up under the locomotion job's execution, helping you identify which stage is consuming the most time. Next Steps The Execution Loop - Understand what each debug stage corresponds to Physics Configuration - Tuning parameters informed by debug data Troubleshooting - Common issues and how debug visualization helps diagnose them

manual/execution-loop.html.title: The Execution Loop | MGM Character Controller
manual/execution-loop.html.summary:
The Execution Loop Every fixed update, the locomotion job processes each character entity through a precise sequence of steps. Understanding this sequence is critical for writing correct state logic. Overview The locomotion job runs as a Burst-compiled IJobChunk scheduled in the FixedStepSimulationSystemGroup, after the physics step. Each character is processed independently and in parallel. The steps execute in this exact order: flowchart TD START(["Frame Start"]) subgraph S1["1. HandleExternalImpulses"] HEI["Accumulate velocity impulses
from CharacterContactSystem"] end subgraph S2["2. Integrate - OnIntegrateState"] INT["Compute velocities + displacement"] end subgraph S3["3. SolveOverlaps"] SO["Multi-plane de-collision
Sets OverlapState: None | Push | Pinch | Crush"] end subgraph S4["4. CollideAndSlide"] CAS_CRUSH{"OverlapState
== Crush?"} CAS_LOOP{"HasRemaining-
Displacement &&
bounce < MaxBounce?"} CAS["OnResolveStateCollisions
Project, cast, slide,
update contacts"] CAS_NEW{"New contact?"} CAS_OC["OnCollision"] CAS_CRUSH -- Yes --> CAS_END CAS_CRUSH -- No --> CAS_LOOP CAS_LOOP -- Yes --> CAS --> CAS_NEW CAS_NEW -- Yes --> CAS_OC --> CAS_LOOP CAS_NEW -- No --> CAS_LOOP CAS_LOOP -- No --> CAS_END CAS_END([done]) end subgraph S5["5. SnapToGround"] SNAP_NONE{"ContactType
== None?"} SNAP_UP{"Moving upward?
dot(vVel, gravity) < −ε"} SNAP_VVEL{"VerticalVelocity
== 0?"} SNAP["Capsule cast downward
Snap position + update contact"] SNAP_NONE -- "Yes (skip)" --> SNAP_END SNAP_NONE -- No --> SNAP_UP SNAP_UP -- "Yes (clear contact)" --> SNAP_END SNAP_UP -- No --> SNAP_VVEL SNAP_VVEL -- "No (skip)" --> SNAP_END SNAP_VVEL -- "Yes (at rest)" --> SNAP --> SNAP_END SNAP_END([done]) end subgraph S6["6. InheritDisplacementFromMotionVelocity"] IH_GROUND{"Grounding
contact?"} IH_CRUSH{"Crush?"} IH_DYN{"Standing on
dynamic body?"} IH["Compute + apply platform displacement
Apply twist rotation"] IH_GROUND -- No --> IH_END IH_GROUND -- Yes --> IH_CRUSH IH_CRUSH -- Yes --> IH_END IH_CRUSH -- No --> IH_DYN IH_DYN -- No --> IH_END IH_DYN -- Yes --> IH --> IH_END IH_END([done]) end subgraph S7["7. EvaluateTransition"] ET["Source-generated dispatch
to LocomotionTransition structs"] end DONE(["Frame End"]) START --> HEI HEI --> INT INT --> SO SO --> CAS_CRUSH CAS_END --> SNAP_NONE SNAP_END --> IH_GROUND IH_END --> ET ET --> DONE Each step in the pipeline is a default interface method (DIM) on ILocomotionStateMachine that can be overridden on your state machine's partial struct. See Customizing the Execution Pipeline for details. There is no OnUpdateState method. The integration and collision resolution phases together form the "update" of your state. Transitions are evaluated once, at the very end, after all physics is resolved. Step-by-Step Breakdown 1. HandleExternalImpulses The CharacterContactSystem runs during the PhysicsSimulationGroup (between contact creation and jacobian creation) and collects velocity impulses from external sources, such as: Dynamic rigid bodies that have collided with the character Other characters pushing against this one Explosions or force fields These impulses are applied to the character's velocity before your state logic runs, so your OnIntegrateState sees the updated velocity. This is why you should not clear velocity unconditionally at the start of integration unless you intend to discard external forces. 2. Integrate (OnIntegrateState) This is where your state logic computes velocities and displacements for the current frame. The system calls your state's OnIntegrateState method. In this step, you should: Compute acceleration from input (movement direction, jump force) Apply gravity acceleration Call baseContext.UpdateVelocitiesAndComputeDisplacements() to convert velocities into displacements What you should NOT do here: Move the character's position directly (that happens in the collision step) Add platform velocity (the ComputeInheritedDisplacement step handles that automatically) Read contact information from this frame (contacts haven't been resolved yet; you see last frame's contacts) The Kinematic Equation UpdateVelocitiesAndComputeDisplacements applies the standard kinematic equations: displacement += velocity * dt + 0.5f * acceleration * dt * dt; velocity += acceleration * dt; This is done separately for horizontal and vertical components, allowing different behavior for each (e.g., gravity only affects vertical, movement input only affects horizontal). 3. SolveOverlaps After integration, the system checks whether the character's collider is currently overlapping with any other collider. This can happen when: A moving platform pushes the character into a wall Another character occupies the same space The character was teleported into geometry The overlap solver attempts to push the character out of the overlap. If it cannot (e.g., the character is pinched between two moving surfaces), it tracks the overlap state: OverlapState Meaning None No overlap detected Push Character was pushed out successfully Pinch Character is squeezed but not fully trapped Crush Character is fully embedded with no escape You can read ctx.BaseContext.OverlapState in your state logic to react to crushing situations (e.g., kill the character). 4. CollideAndSlide (OnResolveStateCollisions + OnCollision) After integration computes the desired displacement, the character needs to actually move through the world while respecting colliders. This is the collide-and-slide loop. The system invokes your state's OnResolveStateCollisions as long as there is remaining displacement, up to CharacterPhysicsProfile.MaxBounce iterations. This multi-bounce approach allows the character to slide along walls, step over obstacles, and navigate complex geometry. After each iteration, if a new contact was detected (the contact changed during Move() and the result is not None), the system calls your state's OnCollision hook. This lets you react to specific surfaces (trampolines, damage zones) without replacing the default collision resolution. See Recipe: Customize Collision Response. Default Behavior If you do not override OnResolveStateCollisions, the default DIM calls baseContext.Move(), which performs two operations: Displacement correction - Adjusts HorizontalDisplacement and VerticalDisplacement based on the current contact type: Contact Type Behavior None Displacement is applied as-is (free movement in air) Ground Vertical displacement is cancelled; horizontal displacement follows the ground slope Wall Step detection is attempted; if not a step, displacement is projected along the wall surface Ceiling Vertical displacement is projected along the ceiling; horizontal displacement is constrained Cast and move - Calls PhysicsOperations.MoveUpToNextObstacle to cast the capsule forward by the corrected displacement, move the character to the hit point (or the full distance if nothing was hit), and update contact information. To customize how collisions are resolved, see Custom Collision Response. Step Detection When a wall contact is detected, the default Move() checks whether it could be a step: The character must be in contact with a surface The slope must exceed MaxSlope The character must be moving into the slope The contact height must be below MaxStepHeight If all conditions are met, PhysicsOperations.TryStep attempts to move the character up and over the step. 5. SnapToGround After all collision resolution is complete, the system performs a downward capsule cast to detect the ground surface below the character. This ensures the character stays grounded when walking down slopes or stairs, rather than becoming airborne for a frame. The snap distance is computed by your context's ComputeGroundSnappingDistance method. The default implementation returns a hardcoded distance of 1f. Override this method on your context's partial struct to account for step height, slope angle, speed, or platform velocity as needed. If a ground surface is found within the snap distance, the character's position is adjusted downward and the contact information is updated. 6. InheritDisplacementFromMotionVelocity If the character is standing on a dynamic body (kinematic or fully-simulated), this step computes the displacement and rotation the character inherits from that body's motion velocity. The displacement is applied to the character's position in the same frame. The inherited velocities are stored in: baseContext.PlatformHorizontalVelocity baseContext.PlatformVerticalVelocity These are available immediately - for example, a jumping state's OnEnterState (called during EvaluateTransition in the same frame) can read PlatformHorizontalVelocity to preserve momentum when jumping off a platform. The position adjustment from platform movement is applied automatically, which is why you should never add platform velocity manually in OnIntegrateState. 7. EvaluateTransition The final step evaluates all [LocomotionTransition] structs registered for the current state. By this point: The character has moved to its final position All contacts are resolved and up-to-date Platform inheritance is computed The source generator dispatches to each transition's ShouldTransition method in the order determined by [EvaluateBefore]/[EvaluateAfter] attributes. The first matching transition wins. The generator automatically handles calling OnExitState on the current state and OnEnterState on the new state. Next Steps Writing Custom States - How to implement each lifecycle method Physics Configuration - Tuning the physics profile Recipe: Override a Pipeline Step - Customize individual steps Recipe: Customize Collision Response - Custom behavior in the collide-and-slide loop

manual/extending.html.title: Extending the Package | MGM Character Controller
manual/extending.html.summary: Extending the Package The MGM Character Controller is designed to be extended without modifying the package source. This page covers common extension points and patterns. Extension Points 1. Custom States The most common extension. Add new movement behaviors (wall running, swimming, climbing, dashing) by creating new state structs and adding them to your state enum. See Writing Custom States for the full guide. 2. Custom Profiles Your ILocomotionProfile struct can contain any unmanaged data. Add fields for new gameplay parameters: [Serializable] public partial struct MyProfile : ILocomotionProfile { public MovingProfile Moving; public FallingProfile Falling; public JumpingProfile Jumping; // Custom extension public SwimmingProfile Swimming; public DashProfile Dash; public WallRunProfile WallRun; } Each sub-profile is a plain struct with configuration fields. The data is baked into a blob asset, so add corresponding fields to your LocomotionProfileAsset and BuildBlobAsset method. 3.
