Custom Context Your context struct can include additional computed properties and helper methods: public partial struct MyContext : ILocomotionContext<MyProfile, MyInput, MyStateData> { public BaseLocomotionContext BaseContext; public MyStateData StateData; // Standard interface properties... // Custom helpers public bool IsUnderwater => BaseContext.CharacterPosition.Position.y < WaterLevel; public bool WantsToMove => Input.MovementMagnitude > math.EPSILON; public bool CanDash => StateData.DashCooldown <= 0; // Custom ground snap for swimming (no ground snap when submerged) public void ComputeGroundSnappingDistance(in MyProfile profile, out float distance) { if (IsUnderwater) { distance = 0; // No ground snapping while swimming return; } // Normal ground snap calculation... } } 4. Custom StateData Add per-state persistent data for any state-specific tracking: public partial struct MyStateData : IComponentData { public JumpData JumpData; public float DashCooldown; public float WallRunTimer; public float3 WallNormal; // Remembered from wall run entry } State data persists across frames and state transitions. Clean it up in OnEnterState or OnExitState as appropriate. 5. Custom Input Add input channels for new actions: public partial struct MyInput : ICharacterLocomotionInput<MyInput> { public float3 Movement; public float JumpTrigger; public float DashTrigger; // New action public float InteractTrigger; // New action public void ApplyInputsToCharacter(in MyInput playerInputs) { Movement = playerInputs.Movement; JumpTrigger += playerInputs.JumpTrigger; // Accumulate triggers DashTrigger += playerInputs.DashTrigger; InteractTrigger += playerInputs.InteractTrigger; } } Remember to read the new inputs in your PlayerInputAsset subclass and consume triggers in your state logic. 6. Custom Ground Snapping Override ComputeGroundSnappingDistance on your context to control when and how far the character snaps to the ground: public void ComputeGroundSnappingDistance(in MyProfile profile, out float distance) { // Disable ground snapping while jumping upward if (StateData.CurrentState == MyStates.Jumping && math.dot(BaseContext.VerticalVelocity, -BaseContext.GravityDirection) > 0) { distance = 0; return; } // Default calculation var slopeDistance = math.length(BaseContext.HorizontalVelocity * BaseContext.TimeStep) * math.tan(math.radians(CharacterPhysicsProfile.MaxSlope)); distance = math.max(CharacterPhysicsProfile.MaxStepHeight, slopeDistance); } 7. Custom Collision Response Override OnResolveStateCollisions to add custom behavior when the character hits something: public void OnResolveStateCollisions(ref MyContext ctx) { BaseLocomotionContext baseContext = ctx.BaseContext; baseContext.Move(); // Custom: bounce off trampolines if (baseContext.IsGroundingContact && HasTrampolineTag(baseContext.LocomotionContact.ValueRO.Entity)) { baseContext.VerticalVelocity = -baseContext.VerticalVelocity * 1.5f; } ctx.BaseContext = baseContext; } 8. Custom Systems You can add systems that modify locomotion components at any point in the pipeline: [UpdateInGroup(typeof(FixedStepSimulationSystemGroup))] [UpdateBefore(typeof(MyLocomotionSystem))] partial struct GravityZoneSystem : ISystem { public void OnUpdate(ref SystemState state) { foreach (var gravity in SystemAPI.Query<RefRW<LocomotionGravity>>() .WithAll<InGravityZone>()) { gravity.ValueRW.Gravity = new float3(0, -4.9f, 0); // Half gravity } } } Customizing the Execution Pipeline Each step in the execution loop is a default interface method (DIM) on ILocomotionStateMachine. You can override any of them on your state machine's partial struct to replace or wrap the default behavior. The source generator produces a partial struct for your state machine (e.g., MyStateMachine). Because it is partial, you can add a second partial declaration that overrides any DIM: public partial struct MyStateMachine { public void Integrate(ref MyStateMachine stateMachine, ref MyContext context) { // Skip movement entirely when crushed if (context.BaseContext.OverlapState == OverlapState.Crush) return; // Otherwise run the default per-state integration stateMachine.IntegratePerState(ref context); } } Overridable Pipeline Methods Method Override to... Execute Replace the entire execution loop HandleExternalImpulses Custom impulse filtering or scaling Integrate Add pre/post integration logic SolveOverlaps Custom overlap resolution CollideAndSlide Custom collision iteration SnapToGround Custom ground adhesion InheritDisplacementFromMotionVelocity Custom platform riding behavior EvaluateTransition Add pre/post transition logic Note IntegratePerState, ResolveCollisionPerState, and EvaluateTransitionPerState are generated by the source generator (switch dispatches to per-state methods). They are not intended to be overridden directly. See The Execution Loop for the ordering and data flow between these steps. Customizing the Locomotion System The source generator creates a partial interface I{Prefix}LocomotionSystem that inherits from ILocomotionSystem<...>. The generated system struct implements this interface with DIM lifecycle methods that you can override. To customize, add your own partial interface declaration: public partial interface IMyLocomotionSystem { void ILocomotionSystem<MyStateMachine, MyStates, MyContext, MyProfile, MyInput, MyStateData> .OnLocomotionSystemUpdate( ref LocomotionJobData jobData, ref EntityQuery query, ref SystemState state) { if (query.IsEmptyIgnoreFilter) return; jobData = jobData.Update(ref state); // Schedule single-threaded instead of parallel (useful for debugging) state.Dependency = ScheduleLocomotionJob( state.Dependency, state.WorldUnmanaged.Time.DeltaTime, jobData, query, jobData.GetPhysicsWorldSingleton()); } } Overridable System Lifecycle Methods Method Override to... OnLocomotionSystemCreate Add RequireForUpdate, custom query filters, rate manager setup OnLocomotionSystemUpdate Use single-threaded scheduling, add conditional skip, integrate rate manager OnLocomotionSystemDestroy Custom resource cleanup Note The default DIM implementations use ref parameters instead of SystemAPI because Unity's source generator cannot patch call sites in code emitted by the package's source generator (both run in parallel). Your own partial interface overrides can use SystemAPI since they are visible to Unity's generator. DIM Customization Reference Complete reference of all default interface methods across the package: Interface.Method Override to... ILocomotionState.OnEnterState Initialize state-specific data on entry ILocomotionState.OnIntegrateState Custom movement logic per state ILocomotionState.OnExitState Clean up state-specific data on exit ILocomotionState.OnResolveStateCollisions Custom collision response per state ILocomotionContext.ComputeGroundSnappingDistance Speed-dependent or state-dependent snap distance ILocomotionStateMachine.Execute Replace the entire execution loop ILocomotionStateMachine.HandleExternalImpulses Custom impulse handling ILocomotionStateMachine.Integrate Pre/post integration logic ILocomotionStateMachine.SolveOverlaps Custom overlap resolution ILocomotionStateMachine.CollideAndSlide Custom collision loop ILocomotionStateMachine.SnapToGround Custom ground adhesion ILocomotionStateMachine.InheritDisplacementFromMotionVelocity Custom platform riding ILocomotionStateMachine.EvaluateTransition Pre/post transition logic ILocomotionSystem.OnLocomotionSystemCreate Custom system initialization ILocomotionSystem.OnLocomotionSystemUpdate Custom scheduling strategy ILocomotionSystem.OnLocomotionSystemDestroy Custom resource cleanup ICharacterLocomotionInput.ApplyInputsToCharacter Accumulate/merge inputs between fixed updates Multiple State Machines You can have multiple independent state machine types in the same project. For example: GroundLocomotionStates - Walking, running, jumping, falling VehicleStates - Driving, braking, boosting, airborne SwimmingStates - Surface, submerged, diving Each has its own enum, context, profile, input, state data, and generated systems. They are completely independent. To switch a character between state machines, you would need to add/remove the relevant components (the state machine component, input, profile, state data). This is an advanced pattern that involves structural entity changes. Working with the Source Generator The Roslyn source generator reads attributes on your anchor struct and state structs and generates: State machine component with dispatch logic Input reader system ISystem lifecycle methods (OnCreate, OnUpdate, OnDestroy) on your locomotion system The generator source is at SourceGenerator/SrcGenProj~/. To modify the generated code patterns: Edit StateMachineSourceGenerator.cs Build with dotnet build from the SrcGenProj~ directory The DLL is copied to SourceGenerator/ and picked up by Unity Warning Modifying the source generator affects all state machines in the project. Test thoroughly. Creating Composite Characters For characters that need both ECS locomotion and complex visual hierarchies, use the CompositeEntityAuthoring pattern: The Locomotor entity carries all physics and state machine components A Model entity follows the Locomotor via CharacterFollower The Model's GameObject can have child objects with Animators, particle systems, audio sources The CharacterFollowerAuthoring links the model to the locomotor during baking. At runtime, the CharacterFollowerSystem syncs the transform. Performance Considerations Scaling to Many Characters The locomotion job runs in parallel across chunks. To maximize throughput: Keep all characters with the same archetype (same component set) Use shared colliders (avoid ForceUniqueColliderAuthoring unless needed) Profile shared blob assets (all characters referencing the same profile share memory) Only enable debug visualization on characters you are actively investigating Burst Compilation State methods run inside a Burst-compiled job, so they must be Burst-compatible. Common mistakes that prevent Burst compilation: Using string or List<T> types Calling managed virtual methods (DIM lifecycle methods are dispatched by the source generator, not via virtual dispatch) Accessing GameObject or MonoBehaviour APIs Using Debug.Log (use [Conditional("ENABLE_UNITY_COLLECTIONS_CHECKS")] guards) Profiling Use Unity's Profiler to identify bottlenecks: The locomotion job appears as LocomotionJob in the Jobs timeline Per-stage profiler markers are static fields on ILocomotionStateMachine, gated by [Conditional("ENABLE_LOCOMOTION_PROFILER")] via ProfilerMarkerExtensions Next Steps Getting Started - Set up your first character Architecture Overview - Understand the full system design Writing Custom States - Implement new movement behaviors Recipe: Override a Pipeline Step - Step-by-step pipeline override Recipe: Run Multiple State Machines - Independent state machines in one project Best Practices - Recommended patterns and anti-patterns

manual/getting-started.html.title: Getting Started | MGM Character Controller
manual/getting-started.html.summary: Getting Started This guide walks you through creating your first character from scratch using the editor scaffolding tool. If you just want to see the package working quickly, start with the Quickstart instead (uses the pre-built sample). Prerequisites MGM Character Controller installed and compiling (Installation) Basic familiarity with Unity ECS concepts (entities, components, systems, baking). If you are new to ECS, review Unity's Entities documentation first. Project Setup Your project needs a sub-scene with physics enabled. The package relies on Unity.Physics for all collision detection and does not use the legacy physics system. Create or open a scene Add a Sub Scene (right-click in Hierarchy > New Sub Scene) Ensure the sub-scene has a Physics Step component (automatically present in most setups) Add colliders to your environment (floor, walls, etc.) Creating Your First Character The fastest way to get started is to use the built-in scaffolding tool: Step 1: Generate the State Machine In the Project window, right-click on the folder where you want to create your state machine Select Assets > Create > WAYN Games > MGM Character Controller > StateMachine Name the folder (e.g., MyCharacter) Press Enter The tool generates a minimal folder structure with the anchor file. Everything else is source-generated at compile time: MyCharacter/ ├── Runtime/ │ ├── mycharacter.runtime.asmdef # Auto-generated assembly definition │ └── MyCharacterStateMachine.cs # Anchor struct with [LocomotionStateMachine] attribute └── Authoring/ └── mycharacter.authoring.asmdef # Auto-generated assembly definition After compilation, the source generator produces all remaining types in memory (context, state enum, profile, input, state data, authoring components, locomotion system, input reader system). You then add states, transitions, and data structs via the Assets > Create > WAYN Games > MGM Character Controller sub-menus. Step 2: Create the Profile Asset In the Project window, right-click Select the Create menu item for your character profile asset (the menu path is generated for you) Configure movement speed, jump force, gravity multiplier, and other parameters in the Inspector Step 3: Create the Input Asset Right-click in the Project window Select the Create menu item for your player input asset Assign your Input Action References for movement and jump Step 4: Set Up the Character Prefab Create a new GameObject in your sub-scene Add the generated Character Locomotor Authoring component Assign your Profile Asset Configure the Physics Profile (capsule height, radius, mass, max slope, etc.) Step 5: Set Up the Player Prefab The Player is a separate concept from the Character. Players own input and control one or more characters. Create a GameObject in the sub-scene (it is baked into a Player entity) Add the generated Player Character Authoring component Assign the Controlled Character (the Locomotor's authoring component), the Input Asset, and the Player GO Prefab (a regular GameObject prefab containing camera and input components, saved outside the sub-scene) Step 6: Enter Play Mode Press Play. Your character should respond to input and interact with the physics environment. If the character falls through the floor, verify that: Your floor has a physics collider The character's collision layers (BelongsTo and CollidesWith) are configured correctly The sub-scene is loaded Importing the Sample The package includes a SinglePlayerSample that demonstrates a complete setup with five states (Idle, Moving, Jumping, Falling, Sliding), a camera rig, moving platforms, and debug visualization. To study the sample: Navigate to Assets/MGM Locomotion/Samples/SinglePlayerSample/ Open the scene in Scenes/ Review the scripts in Scripts/SinglePlayerStateMachine/ for a complete reference implementation Tip The sample is the best resource for understanding how all the pieces connect. Read it alongside this documentation. Next Steps Architecture Overview - Understand the entity types and system design The Execution Loop - Learn what happens each physics tick Writing Custom States - Add new movement behaviors Samples - Study the sample code in detail Troubleshooting - Common setup issues and fixes

manual/hybrid-layer.html.title: Hybrid GameObject Layer | MGM Character Controller
manual/hybrid-layer.html.summary: Hybrid GameObject Layer The Hybrid layer bridges the gap between ECS entities and GameObjects. It enables characters driven by Burst-compiled ECS logic to have animated 3D models, particle effects, audio sources, and cameras, all of which require MonoBehaviour and GameObject APIs. Why a Hybrid Layer? Burst-compiled jobs cannot touch GameObjects, Cameras, Animators, or any managed Unity API. But games need these for rendering, animation, audio, and UI. The hybrid layer solves this by: Running all locomotion logic in pure ECS (fast, parallel, Burst-compatible) Syncing entity transforms to GameObjects each frame via Burst-compiled jobs using TransformAccessArray Letting MonoBehaviours on those GameObjects react to position/rotation changes This means you get the performance of ECS for simulation and the convenience of GameObjects for presentation. Assembly The hybrid layer lives in the WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Hybrid assembly, which references the Runtime assembly. Your game code can reference Hybrid if it needs to interact with the bridge systems. Core Types EntityGameObject (MonoBehaviour) Attached to a GameObject that is linked to an ECS entity. It stores the Entity and World references and registers/unregisters the GameObject's Transform with the singleton transform sync system. When the GameObject is destroyed, its OnDestroy method automatically handles entity cleanup to prevent dangling references. GameObjectEntity (Managed Component) A managed class implementing IComponentData that stores a GameObject Prefab reference, allowing systems to look up the GameObject prefab from the entity side. Because it holds a managed reference, it cannot be used in Burst-compiled jobs. EntityGoCleanup (Managed Cleanup Component) A managed class implementing ICleanupComponentData that stores the GameObject Instance. The EntityGoCleanupSystem processes these to ensure GameObjects are properly disposed when their entities are destroyed. Systems EntityGoInitialisationSystem Runs when new entities with GameObjectEntity components appear. It: Instantiates the referenced GameObject prefab Attaches an EntityGameObject MonoBehaviour to link it back to the entity Registers the transform for syncing EntityGoTransformSyncSystem A system that maintains a static registry of (Entity, Transform) pairs. Each frame, after all ECS jobs complete, it copies LocalToWorld from each registered entity to the corresponding GameObject's Transform. The sync is performed by a Burst-compiled IJobParallelForTransform scheduled via TransformAccessArray, allowing it to run on worker threads rather than the main thread. EntityGoCleanupSystem Processes entities with the EntityGoCleanup component. When an entity is destroyed, this system ensures the associated GameObject is also destroyed, preventing orphaned GameObjects in the scene. EntityGoEditorPreviewSystem (Editor Only) Provides editor-time preview of entity-linked GameObjects. This allows you to see the visual representation in the Scene view without entering Play mode. Character Follower The CharacterFollower component is an ECS component added to "model" entities. It stores a reference to the Locomotor entity that the model should follow. The CharacterFollowerSystem runs each frame and copies the Locomotor's position and rotation to the model entity. The model entity then has its transform synced to the associated GameObject by EntityGoTransformSyncSystem. This two-hop chain (Locomotor -> Model Entity -> GameObject) ensures: The Locomotor runs in parallel with no GameObject dependencies The Model entity can apply visual offsets or smoothing The GameObject receives the final visual transform Player GameObject Management PlayerGoManagerSystem This system manages the lifecycle of player GameObjects: Spawn: When a new Player entity appears with a PlayerGameObjectPrefab but no PlayerGameObjectInstance, the system instantiates the prefab and stores the instance reference Destroy: When a Player entity is destroyed, the system destroys the associated GameObject The player GameObject typically contains: A Camera component A CinemachineBrain for camera blending A PlayerInput component for input handling A PlayerGoAuthoring with references to the camera, camera target, and follow strategy CameraRigFollowControlledCharacterSystem Ensures the player's camera rig follows the controlled character. It maintains a Singleton component with registered player entities and camera transforms, then copies each player entity's LocalToWorld position to the corresponding camera rig transform. DestructionManager A MonoBehaviour that manages delayed destruction of GameObjects. It invokes a configurable UnityEvent before destroying the GameObject after an optional delay. Entity cleanup is handled separately by EntityGameObject.OnDestroy(). For a step-by-step setup guide, see Recipe: Set Up a Hybrid Character. Next Steps Architecture Overview - How the entity types relate Input System - How player input flows through the system Integration: Animation - Connecting an Animator to a hybrid character Integration: Cinemachine - Camera setup on player GameObjects

manual/input-system.html.title: Input System | MGM Character Controller
manual/input-system.html.summary:
Input System The MGM Character Controller separates input reading from input consumption. This design supports multiple input sources (keyboard, gamepad, AI, network), handles frame rate differences between Update and FixedUpdate, and works cleanly with Unity's new Input System. Architecture Input flows through three layers: flowchart LR subgraph L1["1. Read"] A["PlayerInputAsset
MonoBehaviour · ScriptableObject
Reads hardware input
Update rate"] end subgraph L2["2. Accumulate"] B["ICharacterLocomotionInput
ECS component
ApplyInputsToCharacter()
Update rate"] end subgraph L3["3. Consume"] C["State logic
Burst job
Reads TInput on context
FixedUpdate rate"] end A -- "ReadInputsFromAsset(out TInput)" --> B B -- "generated InputReaderSystem" --> C Why Three Layers? Frame rate mismatch: Unity's Update loop runs at variable rate (e.g., 60-144 fps), but FixedUpdate runs at a fixed rate (e.g., 50 fps). If a player presses Jump during a frame where no FixedUpdate runs, that press must not be lost. The accumulation layer ensures trigger inputs survive until the next fixed step. Burst compatibility: PlayerInputAsset is a ScriptableObject that touches MonoBehaviour APIs (Camera, PlayerInput, Input System). These cannot run inside Burst jobs. The separation allows the managed-code layer to read input and the Burst-compiled layer to consume it. Multiple input sources: By abstracting input behind ICharacterLocomotionInput, you can swap the input source without changing any state logic. A keyboard player, a gamepad player, and an AI agent all write to the same TInput component. PlayerInputAsset PlayerInputAsset is an abstract ScriptableObject that you subclass to define how hardware input maps to your input struct. [CreateAssetMenu(menuName = "My Game/Player Input Asset")] public class MyPlayerInputAsset : PlayerInputAsset { public InputActionReference MovementAction; public InputActionReference JumpAction; Camera _camera; PlayerInput _playerInput; public override void ReadInputsFromAsset(out MyInput inputs) { // Read raw input var moveRaw = _playerInput.actions[MovementAction.name].ReadValue(); // Convert to world-space relative to camera Transform cam = _camera.transform; float3 worldMove = cam.forward * moveRaw.y + cam.right * moveRaw.x; worldMove -= math.projectsafe(worldMove, math.up()); // Remove vertical component inputs.Movement = math.normalizesafe(worldMove); // Read trigger inputs.JumpTrigger = _playerInput.actions[JumpAction.name].WasPerformedThisFrame() ? 1f : 0f; } public override void InitInputAsset(PlayerGameObjectInstance instance) { var playerGo = instance.Instance.Value; _camera = playerGo.GetComponent().Camera; _playerInput = playerGo.GetComponent(); _playerInput.ActivateInput(); } } Key points ReadInputsFromAsset is called every Update frame (not FixedUpdate). Return a fresh TInput each call. InitInputAsset is called once when the player is instantiated. Use it to cache references to the camera, PlayerInput, and any other managed objects. The asset is instantiated per player at runtime (ScriptableObject.Instantiate), so each player gets their own cached references. Multiple players on the same machine each have their own copy. Camera-Relative Input Most 3D games convert 2D stick/keyboard input to world-space movement relative to the camera. The pattern is: Read the 2D input vector Multiply by the camera's forward and right vectors Project onto the horizontal plane (remove the gravity-axis component) Normalize This ensures "push up on the stick" always means "move away from the camera," regardless of camera angle. ICharacterLocomotionInput Your input struct must implement this interface: public struct MyInput : ICharacterLocomotionInput { public float3 Movement; public float JumpTrigger; public void ApplyInputsToCharacter(in MyInput playerInputs) { // Continuous inputs: overwrite with latest value Movement = playerInputs.Movement; // Trigger inputs: ACCUMULATE, don't overwrite JumpTrigger += playerInputs.JumpTrigger; } } Input Accumulation The critical distinction is between continuous and trigger inputs: Type Example Behavior Why Continuous Movement direction, aim angle Overwrite each frame Only the latest value matters Trigger Jump press, fire button Accumulate across frames A press during a non-FixedUpdate frame must not be lost If you overwrite JumpTrigger instead of accumulating it, and the player presses Jump during a frame where no FixedUpdate runs, the press is silently discarded. Consuming Triggers In your state's OnIntegrateState, after reading the trigger value, reset it to zero: internal static void OnIntegrateState(ref MyContext ctx) { if (ctx.Input.JumpTrigger > 0) { // Apply jump Jump(ref ctx); } // Consume the trigger MyInput input = ctx.Input; input.JumpTrigger = 0; ctx.Input = input; } Generated Input Reader System The source generator creates an input reader system that: Runs each Update frame (not FixedUpdate) For each player, calls PlayerInputAsset.ReadInputsFromAsset to get raw input For each character controlled by that player, calls ICharacterLocomotionInput.ApplyInputsToCharacter to write to the ECS component You never need to write or modify this system. It is produced automatically from your [LocomotionStateMachine] annotation. AI and Network Input Because input is just an ECS component, you can drive characters from any source: AI Input Create a system that writes directly to the TInput component without going through PlayerInputAsset: [BurstCompile] partial struct AIInputSystem : ISystem { [BurstCompile] public void OnUpdate(ref SystemState state) { foreach (var (input, transform) in SystemAPI.Query, RefRO>() .WithAll()) { // AI decision-making logic input.ValueRW.Movement = ComputeAIMovement(transform.ValueRO); input.ValueRW.JumpTrigger = ShouldJump() ? 1f : 0f; } } } Network Input For networked games, the client sends input to the server, which writes it to the TInput component. The locomotion job runs identically on both client and server, producing deterministic results (assuming the same input and physics state). Cinemachine Integration The sample's LocomotionPlayerInputAsset demonstrates Cinemachine camera setup: Find all CinemachineVirtualCameraBase components on the player prefab Set their Follow and LookAt targets to the character's camera target transform Set a camera override on the CinemachineBrain to blend between the player's virtual cameras Assign CinemachineInputAxisController player indices for per-player camera control This ensures each player in a split-screen setup has their own camera pipeline. Next Steps Hybrid GameObject Layer - How player GameObjects are managed Writing Custom States - Consuming input in state logic Integration: Cinemachine - Camera setup with Cinemachine Recipe: Switch to AI Input - Drive characters from AI systems

manual/install.html.title: Installation | MGM Character Controller
manual/install.html.summary: Installation This page covers installing the MGM Character Controller package and verifying that everything is working. Prerequisites Unity 6000.3 or later A project configured for DOTS (Entities, Unity.Physics, Burst, Jobs) If you are new to Unity ECS, review Unity's Entities documentation first. You do not need to be an ECS expert, but you should understand the basic concepts of entities, components, systems, and baking. Install from the Asset Store Purchase or download the package from the Unity Asset Store Open your Unity project Go to Window > Package Manager Select My Assets in the dropdown Find MGM Character Controller and click Import Install from a Local Folder Open your Unity project Go to Window > Package Manager Click the + button and select Add package from disk... Navigate to the package.json file inside the package folder Click Open Install from a Git URL Open Window > Package Manager Click + > Add package from git URL... Enter the repository URL Verify Installation After installation, confirm the package is working: Open Window > Package Manager Find MGM Character Controller in the package list Verify the version shows 0.5.0 Check the Console for any compilation errors If you see errors related to missing Entities or Physics packages, install the required dependencies: Package Minimum Version com.unity.entities 1.3.9 com.unity.physics 1.3.8 com.unity.burst 1.8.18 com.unity.collections 2.5.4 com.unity.mathematics 1.3.2 Next Step Continue to the Quickstart to see a character running in minutes.

manual/integrations/animation.html.title: Integration: Animation | MGM Character Controller
manual/integrations/animation.html.summary: Integration: Animation Characters driven by the MGM Character Controller use a separate Model entity with an attached GameObject for rendering and animation. The Animator component lives on this GameObject, not on the ECS entity. Architecture Locomotor Entity (ECS) → CharacterFollower → Model Entity (ECS) → GameObject (Animator) The Locomotor entity runs state machine logic in Burst jobs The Model entity follows the Locomotor via CharacterFollowerSystem The Model's GameObject gets its transform synced via EntityGoTransformSyncSystem A MonoBehaviour on the GameObject reads entity state and drives the Animator Setup 1. Create the model GameObject In the sub-scene: Create a child GameObject with your character mesh and Animator Add a CharacterFollowerAuthoring component Set the Locomotor field to reference the character's Locomotor authoring component 2. Create an animation bridge MonoBehaviour Since Burst jobs cannot touch the Animator, you need a MonoBehaviour bridge that reads ECS state and sets Animator parameters: public class LocomotionAnimationBridge : MonoBehaviour { Animator _animator; EntityGameObject _entityLink; // Animator parameter hashes static readonly int SpeedHash = Animator.StringToHash("Speed"); static readonly int IsGroundedHash = Animator.StringToHash("IsGrounded"); static readonly int IsJumpingHash = Animator.StringToHash("IsJumping"); void Awake() { _animator = GetComponent<Animator>(); _entityLink = GetComponent<EntityGameObject>(); } void LateUpdate() { if (_entityLink == null || !_entityLink.IsValid) return; // Read entity data from the ECS world var entityManager = World.DefaultGameObjectInjectionWorld.EntityManager; var entity = _entityLink.Entity; // Read velocity for speed parameter if (entityManager.HasComponent<LocomotionVelocity>(entity)) { var velocity = entityManager.GetComponentData<LocomotionVelocity>(entity); _animator.SetFloat(SpeedHash, math.length(velocity.Value)); } // Read contact for grounded state if (entityManager.HasComponent<LocomotionContact>(entity)) { var contact = entityManager.GetComponentData<LocomotionContact>(entity); _animator.SetBool(IsGroundedHash, contact.ContactType == ContactType.Ground); } } } 3. Attach to the model GameObject Add LocomotionAnimationBridge to the same GameObject that has the Animator. The EntityGameObject component is added automatically by the hybrid layer during initialization. State-Aware Animation To read the current state machine state, you need access to the generated state machine component. Since this is a generic type, you can either: Option A: Use a managed component or shared data structure to expose the current state to MonoBehaviours. Option B: Create a system that writes animation-relevant data to a dedicated "animation data" component readable from the bridge. Option B is recommended because it keeps the main thread work minimal: public struct AnimationData : IComponentData { public float Speed; public bool IsGrounded; public bool IsJumping; public bool IsFalling; } [UpdateAfter(typeof(MyLocomotionSystem))] partial struct AnimationDataSystem : ISystem { public void OnUpdate(ref SystemState state) { foreach (var (animData, velocity, contact) in SystemAPI.Query<RefRW<AnimationData>, RefRO<LocomotionVelocity>, RefRO<LocomotionContact>>()) { animData.ValueRW.Speed = math.length(velocity.ValueRO.Value); animData.ValueRW.IsGrounded = contact.ValueRO.ContactType == ContactType.Ground; } } } Known Caveats Animation is always one frame behind physics because the Animator runs after ECS syncs the transform Root motion is not supported - the Locomotor entity drives position, not the Animator Blending between states should be handled by the Animator Controller, not by the locomotion state machine Next Steps Hybrid GameObject Layer - How entity-to-GameObject sync works Architecture Overview - Locomotor vs Model entity separation

manual/integrations/cinemachine.html.title: Integration: Cinemachine | MGM Character Controller
manual/integrations/cinemachine.html.summary: Integration: Cinemachine The MGM Character Controller works with Cinemachine for third-person (and other) camera setups. The Player prefab holds the camera rig, and the system automatically connects it to the controlled character. Setup 1. Player prefab The Player GO Prefab should contain: A Camera component A CinemachineBrain on the same camera GameObject One or more Cinemachine virtual cameras (e.g., CinemachineCamera for third-person follow) A PlayerGoAuthoring MonoBehaviour with references to the camera and a camera target transform 2. Virtual camera targeting In your PlayerInputAsset.InitInputAsset, connect the virtual cameras to the character's camera target: public override void InitInputAsset(PlayerGameObjectInstance instance) { var playerGo = instance.Instance.Value; var playerGoAuthoring = playerGo.GetComponent<PlayerGoAuthoring>(); _camera = playerGoAuthoring.Camera; _playerInput = playerGo.GetComponent<PlayerInput>(); // Connect Cinemachine cameras to the character's follow target var cameraTarget = playerGoAuthoring.CameraTarget; var virtualCameras = playerGo.GetComponentsInChildren<CinemachineVirtualCameraBase>(); foreach (var vc in virtualCameras) { vc.Follow = cameraTarget; vc.LookAt = cameraTarget; } } 3. Camera target tracking The CameraRigFollowControlledCharacterSystem updates the camera target position to match the controlled character's position each frame. This happens automatically when the Player entity has a ControlledCharacters buffer. 4. Camera-relative input In ReadInputsFromAsset, convert 2D input to world-space relative to the camera: Transform cam = _camera.transform; float3 worldMove = cam.forward * moveRaw.y + cam.right * moveRaw.x; worldMove -= math.projectsafe(worldMove, math.up()); inputs.Movement = math.normalizesafe(worldMove); Local Multiplayer For split-screen setups: Each Player entity gets its own Player GO Prefab instance Each instance has its own Camera + CinemachineBrain CinemachineInputAxisController components should have their PlayerIndex set to match the PlayerInput component's player index The PlayerInputAsset is instantiated per player, so each player caches their own camera reference. Known Caveats The camera target transform is a GameObject synced via CameraRigFollowControlledCharacterSystem, which uses a Burst-compiled IJobParallelForTransform with TransformAccessArray. Camera lag during fast movement can occur if the sync runs after Cinemachine's update. Ensure the sync system runs before Cinemachine evaluates. Next Steps Input System - Camera-relative input in depth Hybrid GameObject Layer - How player GameObjects are managed

manual/moving-platforms.html.title: Moving Platforms | MGM Character Controller
manual/moving-platforms.html.summary: Moving Platforms The MGM Character Controller has built-in support for moving and rotating platforms. Characters standing on platforms automatically inherit their velocity and rotation without any special logic in your state code. How It Works Moving platforms are kinematic physics bodies. The locomotion system detects when a character is grounded on any dynamic body (kinematic or fully-simulated) and automatically computes the displacement and rotation the character should inherit. Contact Detection During the SnapToGround step, the system detects that the character is standing on a dynamic body. It records the body index and entity reference in the LocomotionContact component. InheritDisplacementFromMotionVelocity At the end of the locomotion job, the InheritDisplacementFromMotionVelocity step reads the body's motion velocity from the physics world and decomposes it into: PlatformHorizontalVelocity - The body's velocity projected onto the horizontal plane PlatformVerticalVelocity - The body's velocity along the gravity axis The displacement is applied to the character's position in the same frame. The platform rotation is applied directly via a twist quaternion computation. The inherited velocities are available immediately in the same frame's EvaluateTransition step (e.g., a jumping state's OnEnterState can read PlatformHorizontalVelocity to preserve momentum when jumping off a platform). Important You should never add platform velocity manually in OnIntegrateState. The system handles it automatically. Adding it yourself would result in double-application, making the character move twice as fast on platforms. Platform Components PlatformMover Stores the movement speed and current waypoint index for track-based platform movement. Updated by the PlatformMovementSystem. Rotor Stores angular velocity for rotating platforms. The system applies the rotation each fixed update. Track Constrains platform motion along a predefined path. The platform loops cyclically through waypoints. For a step-by-step setup guide, see Recipe: Set Up a Moving Platform. PlatformMovementSystem The PlatformMovementSystem runs in the BeforePhysicsSystemGroup and computes the desired linear and angular velocities based on PlatformMover and Rotor components. It writes to the PhysicsVelocity component only - Unity.Physics then integrates those velocities to update the platform's transform during the physics step. Character Behavior on Platforms Standing Still When idle on a platform, the character moves with the platform. If the platform rotates, the character's rotation is updated directly by the InheritDisplacementFromMotionVelocity step via a twist quaternion computation (see the sample's IdleState.OnIntegrateState for the spin/roll/slide interpolation logic). Moving on a Platform When walking on a moving platform, the character's own velocity is additive with the platform's velocity. The character moves relative to the platform surface. Jumping off a Platform When the character jumps, the JumpingState.OnEnterState adds PlatformHorizontalVelocity to the character's horizontal velocity: ctx.BaseContext.HorizontalVelocity += ctx.BaseContext.PlatformHorizontalVelocity; This preserves the platform's momentum, so jumping off a fast-moving platform launches the character in the direction the platform was moving. Without this, the character would appear to "snap back" to a standstill mid-air. Platform Push (Overlap Resolution) If a platform moves into the character (e.g., a rising elevator ceiling), the SolveOverlaps step pushes the character out. If the character is trapped between two surfaces (e.g., elevator ceiling and floor), the overlap state transitions through Push -> Pinch -> Crush. When the character is pushed into geometry, the collide-and-slide algorithm can still move it back out because backface collisions are ignored. This means a character that has been shoved inside a wall by a platform can escape by moving away from the wall - the capsule cast will not collide with the interior face of the geometry. Custom Platform Behaviors You can create custom platform movement by writing your own system that modifies the PhysicsVelocity component on platform entities. The locomotion system will automatically handle character inheritance for any dynamic body the character is grounded on. Important Your system must run in BeforePhysicsSystemGroup so that the velocities are set before Unity.Physics integrates them into the platform's transform. For example, a platform that follows a spline: [BurstCompile] [UpdateInGroup(typeof(BeforePhysicsSystemGroup))] partial struct SplinePlatformSystem : ISystem { [BurstCompile] public void OnUpdate(ref SystemState state) { float dt = SystemAPI.Time.fixedDeltaTime; foreach (var (velocity, spline, transform) in SystemAPI.Query<RefRW<PhysicsVelocity>, RefRW<SplineFollower>, RefRO<LocalTransform>>() ) { float3 targetPos = EvaluateSpline(spline.ValueRO, SystemAPI.Time.ElapsedTime); velocity.ValueRW.Linear = (targetPos - transform.ValueRO.Position) / dt; } } } Next Steps The Execution Loop - Understand the platform-related steps Physics Configuration - Platform-relevant physics settings Recipe: Per-Character Gravity - Gravity wells and custom gravity zones

manual/physics-configuration.html.title: Physics Configuration | MGM Character Controller
manual/physics-configuration.html.summary: Physics Configuration The CharacterPhysicsProfile defines the physical properties of the character's collision capsule and how it interacts with the world. This page explains each parameter, its effect, and how to tune it. CharacterPhysicsProfile Fields Capsule Geometry Field Type Description CapsuleHeight float Total height of the collision capsule from bottom to top, in world units. The capsule's bottom sphere sits at y = CapsuleRadius and the top sphere at y = CapsuleHeight - CapsuleRadius. CapsuleRadius float Radius of both hemisphere caps. Also determines the minimum width of the character. Default: 0.5. The capsule geometry directly affects: Which gaps the character can fit through How close the character can get to walls Step detection (contact height relative to capsule bottom) Mass and Forces Field Type Description Mass float Weight of the character. Used for collision response calculations when the character collides with dynamic rigid bodies. Higher mass means the character pushes objects more. MassToDynamic float Multiplier applied to all impulses the character exerts on dynamic bodies during collision response. Movement Limits Field Type Description MaxSlope float Maximum slope angle (in degrees) the character can walk up. Surfaces steeper than this are treated as walls. Typical values: 45-60 degrees. MaxStepHeight float Maximum height (in world units) of obstacles the character can automatically step over. Typical values: 0.3-0.5. MaxBounce int Maximum number of collide-and-slide iterations per frame. Each bounce allows the character to redirect along a surface it hits. Higher values handle more complex geometry (corners, narrow corridors) but cost more. Default: 3. Collision Layers Field Type Description BelongsTo LayerMask The physics layer(s) this character belongs to. Other colliders check this to decide if they can collide with the character. CollidesWith LayerMask The physics layer(s) this character tests against. The character only detects collisions with colliders on these layers. To make characters ignore each other, put them on a layer that does not appear in other characters' CollidesWith mask. To make characters collide with each other, include the character layer in both masks. Collision Tolerance Field Type Description CollisionTolerance float Skin thickness for all collision queries. This is the distance at which the system considers two surfaces "in contact." Decrease for tighter collision response; increase if you observe jitter or tunneling. Defaults to CollisionWorld.DefaultCollisionTolerance. Interpolation Field Type Description InterpolationMode enum Interpolated enables Unity.Physics graphical smoothing between fixed updates, producing smoother visual movement. NotInterpolated updates the transform directly each fixed step, which can appear jittery at low fixed rates but is simpler to debug. Default Values The static CharacterPhysicsProfile.Default property provides sensible starting values: CharacterPhysicsProfile.Default = new CharacterPhysicsProfile { MaxBounce = 3, CapsuleRadius = 0.5f, CapsuleHeight = 2f, BelongsTo = 1, // Layer 0 (Default) CollidesWith = ~0, // All layers CollisionTolerance = CollisionWorld.DefaultCollisionTolerance }; Collision Response The character's capsule collider is created with CollisionResponsePolicy.CollideRaiseCollisionEvents. This means: The collider participates in collision detection Collision events are raised, allowing CharacterContactSystem to intercept contacts However, CollideRaiseCollisionEvents alone would still let Unity.Physics solve the contact normally. The package prevents this by setting JacobianFlags.Disabled on every contact that involves a character inside CharacterContactSystem. This disables the physics solver's constraint for that contact, giving the locomotion job full control over the character's movement. The system then records the contact data (penetration depth, surface normal, velocity) for use by the locomotion pipeline's SolveOverlaps and HandleExternalImpulses steps. Shared vs Unique Colliders By default, capsule colliders are shared across entities with identical geometry and filter settings. This saves memory when you have many characters with the same shape. If you need a unique collider per entity (e.g., for runtime shape modification), add a ForceUniqueColliderAuthoring component to the character's authoring GameObject. Tuning Guide Character walks through walls Increase MaxBounce (try 5-6, default is 3) Check CollidesWith includes the wall's layer Increase CollisionTolerance slightly Character jitters on the ground Enable InterpolationMode.Interpolated Check that CollisionTolerance is not too small Verify the ground collider is static (not rebuilding each frame) Character cannot climb steps Increase MaxStepHeight Ensure the step geometry is clean (not sloped mesh artifacts) Check that MaxSlope is high enough that the step face is classified as a wall Character slides off slopes it should walk on Increase MaxSlope (the threshold between "ground" and "wall") Character clips through fast descending platforms The ground snapping distance may be too small for platforms moving downward faster than the snap can track. Override ComputeGroundSnappingDistance in your context to account for higher vertical platform speeds How the Collider Is Built During baking, the BaseCharacterLocomotorBaker constructs the capsule collider from your profile: Creates CapsuleGeometry from CapsuleHeight and CapsuleRadius Creates CollisionFilter from BelongsTo and CollidesWith Sets CollisionResponsePolicy.CollideRaiseCollisionEvents Hashes the geometry + filter to check if a matching blob already exists If not, creates a new CapsuleCollider blob asset Attaches it as a PhysicsCollider component Gravity The LocomotionGravity Component Gravity is stored per-entity in the LocomotionGravity component, initialized to PhysicsStep.Default.Gravity (typically (0, -9.81, 0)) during baking. The component provides two computed properties: GravityDirection - Normalized direction vector (usually (0, -1, 0)) GravityStrength - Magnitude (usually 9.81) You can modify the LocomotionGravity component at runtime to change gravity per-character. See Recipe: Per-Character Gravity. How Gravity Affects the Pipeline Gravity is not just a force applied during integration - it defines the character's entire reference frame. The GravityDirection determines what "up", "horizontal", and "vertical" mean for every step of the locomotion pipeline: Character orientation - At the start of each frame, the locomotion job reorients the character's rotation so that "up" is opposite the gravity direction. This happens before any state logic runs. Velocity decomposition - The character's velocity is split into HorizontalVelocity (perpendicular to gravity) and VerticalVelocity (along gravity). All state logic works with these decomposed values, not raw world-space velocity. Contact classification - Surfaces are classified as Ground, Wall, or Ceiling based on the angle between their normal and the gravity direction: Ground: normal-to-gravity angle below MaxSlope Wall: angle above MaxSlope but not facing away from gravity Ceiling: normal points in the same general direction as gravity Displacement correction - The Move() method projects displacement along surfaces relative to gravity. Ground contact cancels vertical displacement; wall contact projects horizontal displacement along the wall plane defined relative to gravity. Capsule orientation - All physics casts (collide-and-slide, snap-to-ground, step detection) position the capsule along the gravity axis, not world up. Snap-to-ground - The downward capsule cast is performed along GravityDirection, not (0, -1, 0). Platform inheritance - Platform velocity is split into horizontal and vertical components relative to the contact surface normal. Input and Gravity Movement input should be projected onto the plane perpendicular to gravity. The sample's MovingState demonstrates this: float3 forwardOnPlane = movementDirection - math.projectsafe(movementDirection, -baseContext.GravityDirection); forwardOnPlane = math.normalizesafe(forwardOnPlane); This ensures "forward" always means "forward along the ground" regardless of gravity direction. Using Gravity in State Logic Your state's OnIntegrateState reads gravity from the context to compute vertical acceleration: float3 gravityAcceleration = baseContext.GravityDirection * baseContext.GravityStrength; baseContext.UpdateVelocitiesAndComputeDisplacements(moveAcceleration, gravityAcceleration); Jump force is applied opposite to gravity: baseContext.VerticalVelocity = jumpForce * -baseContext.GravityDirection; Because the entire pipeline is gravity-relative, changing LocomotionGravity at runtime automatically adapts contact classification, displacement projection, capsule orientation, and ground snapping - no state logic changes needed. Next Steps Writing Custom States - Using physics data in state logic Moving Platforms - Platform velocity inheritance Troubleshooting - Common physics-related issues Recipe: Customize Ground Snapping - Advanced ground snap configuration Recipe: Per-Character Gravity - Gravity wells and custom gravity zones

manual/quickstart.html.title: Quickstart | MGM Character Controller
manual/quickstart.html.summary: Quickstart This guide gets you to a running character as fast as possible using the included sample. Goal: A capsule character that walks, jumps, and rides moving platforms. Time: Under 5 minutes. Prerequisites MGM Character Controller installed (Installation) An empty or existing Unity project with DOTS packages Step 1: Import the Sample Open Window > Package Manager Select MGM Character Controller in the package list Expand the Samples section Click Import next to SinglePlayerSample The sample is imported into Assets/Samples/MGM Character Controller/0.5.0/SinglePlayerSample/. Step 2: Create a Scene The sample provides scripts, prefabs, and assets but not a scene. You need to create one: Create a new scene (File > New Scene > Basic (Built-in)) Save it anywhere in your project Step 3: Set Up the Sub-Scene Right-click in the Hierarchy and select New Sub Scene > Empty Scene Inside the sub-scene, create a floor: 3D Object > Plane Scale the plane to (10, 1, 10) so there is room to move Add a collider to the plane Step 4: Add the Character Inside the sub-scene, create an empty GameObject named Character Add the Locomotion Character Locomotor Authoring component (search for "LocomotionCharacterLocomotor" in Add Component) Assign the SinglePlayerProfileAsset from the imported sample (Assets/Samples/.../SinglePlayerSample/Assets/SinglePlayerProfileAsset) The physics profile defaults are fine for now (2m tall capsule, 0.5m radius) Step 5: Add the Player Inside the sub-scene, create another empty GameObject named Player Add the Locomotion Player Character Authoring component (search for "LocomotionPlayerCharacter" in Add Component) Set the Character field to reference the Character GameObject from Step 4 Assign the SinglePlayerInputAsset from the imported sample Assign the Player (Prefab) from the imported sample as the Player GO Prefab Step 6: Press Play Press Play. You should see: A capsule character standing on the plane WASD / left stick moves the character Space / gamepad south button makes the character jump The camera follows the character If Something Went Wrong Problem Fix Character falls through the floor Make sure the plane has a collider component Character doesn't move Check that the SinglePlayerInputAsset is assigned on the Player and that Unity's Input System package is installed No camera Check that the Player (Prefab) is assigned on the Player entity Console errors about missing components Make sure you are using Unity 6000.3+ and all DOTS packages are installed What Just Happened? Three entities were created during baking: Locomotor - The character entity running in pure ECS. A Burst-compiled job processes it each fixed update through a 7-step pipeline: overlap resolution, impulse handling, integration (your state logic), collide-and-slide, ground snapping, platform inheritance, and transition evaluation. Player - Owns the input asset and camera. The PlayerGOManagerSystem instantiated the player prefab (camera + input) at runtime. Model - Follows the Locomotor via CharacterFollower and syncs its transform to a GameObject for rendering. The sample defines five states - Idle, Moving, Jumping, Falling, and Sliding - that cover basic character locomotion. Transitions between states are declared as small structs with [LocomotionTransition] attributes. Next Steps Getting Started - Create your own character from scratch with the scaffolding tool Architecture Overview - Understand the entity types and data flow Writing Custom States - Add new movement behaviors Samples - Study order for the sample code

manual/recipes/add-a-new-state.html.title: Recipe: Add a New State | MGM Character Controller
manual/recipes/add-a-new-state.html.summary: Recipe: Add a New State Add a new movement behavior (e.g., Dashing) to an existing state machine. When to Use You want a new movement mode that is distinct from existing states (different physics, different input handling, different transitions). Prerequisites An existing state machine created with the scaffolding tool or manually Familiarity with Writing Custom States Steps 1. Generate the state file Right-click in the Project window Select Assets > Create > WAYN Games > MGM Character Controller > State Name it (e.g., Dashing) Press Enter This creates a new file DashingState.cs and adds the enum entry with the [LocomotionState] attribute. 2. Add profile data (if needed) If the new state needs tunable parameters, add a sub-profile: [Serializable] public struct DashProfile { public float DashSpeed; public float DashDuration; public float DashCooldown; } Add it to your main profile struct: public partial struct MyProfile : ILocomotionProfile { public MovingProfile Moving; public FallingProfile Falling; public JumpingProfile Jumping; public DashProfile Dash; // New } Update your LocomotionProfileAsset.BuildBlobAsset to copy the new fields. 3. Add state data (if needed) If the state needs to track data across frames (timers, counters), add fields to your state data struct: public partial struct MyStateData : IComponentData { public JumpData JumpData; public float DashTimer; // New public float DashCooldown; // New } 4. Implement the state logic [LocomotionState(typeof(MyStateMachine))] internal partial struct DashingState { public void OnEnterState(ref MyContext ctx) { // Set initial dash velocity in the current facing direction ctx.BaseContext.HorizontalVelocity = math.forward(ctx.BaseContext.CharacterPosition.Rotation) * ctx.Profile.Dash.DashSpeed; ctx.BaseContext.VerticalVelocity = float3.zero; ctx.StateData.DashTimer = ctx.Profile.Dash.DashDuration; } public void OnIntegrateState(ref MyContext ctx) { ref BaseLocomotionContext baseContext = ref ctx.BaseContext; // Maintain dash velocity, no acceleration baseContext.UpdateVelocitiesAndComputeDisplacements(); ctx.StateData.DashTimer -= baseContext.TimeStep; } public void OnExitState(ref MyContext ctx) { ctx.StateData.DashCooldown = ctx.Profile.Dash.DashCooldown; } } 5. Add transitions Create transition structs to enter and exit the new state: [LocomotionTransition(typeof(MovingState), typeof(DashingState))] [EvaluateBefore(typeof(MovingToJumping))] // Higher priority than jump public struct MovingToDashing : IMyTransition { public bool ShouldTransition(ref MyContext ctx) => ctx.Input.DashTrigger > 0 && ctx.StateData.DashCooldown <= 0; } [LocomotionTransition(typeof(DashingState), typeof(MovingState))] public struct DashingToMoving : IMyTransition { public bool ShouldTransition(ref MyContext ctx) => ctx.StateData.DashTimer <= 0; } [LocomotionTransition(typeof(DashingState), typeof(FallingState))] [EvaluateBefore(typeof(DashingToMoving))] public struct DashingToFalling : IMyTransition { public bool ShouldTransition(ref MyContext ctx) => !ctx.BaseContext.IsInContact; } 6. Add input (if needed) If the state requires a new input action (e.g., dash trigger), add it to your input struct and PlayerInputAsset. See Input System. Expected Result The character dashes forward at high speed when the dash trigger is pressed, maintains that velocity for the configured duration, then transitions back to moving (or falling if airborne). Common Mistakes Using managed types (strings, classes, arrays) in state logic - states run inside Burst-compiled jobs Not consuming trigger inputs (dash fires every frame) Related Writing Custom States - Full lifecycle reference Recipe: Add a New Transition

manual/recipes/add-a-new-transition.html.title: Recipe: Add a New Transition | MGM Character Controller
manual/recipes/add-a-new-transition.html.summary: Recipe: Add a New Transition Add a transition between two existing states using the [LocomotionTransition] attribute pattern. When to Use You have two states and need to define when and how the character moves from one to the other. Prerequisites Both states exist in your state machine Familiarity with Writing Custom States Steps 1. Create the transition struct Right-click in the Project window Select Assets > Create > WAYN Games > MGM Character Controller > Transition Name it (e.g., MovingToSliding) Press Enter This creates a new file with the [LocomotionTransition] attribute. Update the generated struct to implement the generated I{Prefix}Transition interface and add your condition: [LocomotionTransition(typeof(MovingState), typeof(SlidingState))] public struct MovingToSliding : IMyTransition { public bool ShouldTransition(ref MyContext ctx) => ctx.BaseContext.IsSlopeAboveMax && !ctx.BaseContext.IsCeiling; } Key points: [LocomotionTransition(typeof(FromState), typeof(ToState))] tells the source generator to discover this transition and declares the source and target states ShouldTransition returns true when the transition should fire The source generator handles calling OnExitState on the source and OnEnterState on the target 2. Set priority with ordering attributes If multiple transitions can fire from the same source state, control which evaluates first: [LocomotionTransition(typeof(MovingState), typeof(JumpingState))] [EvaluateBefore(typeof(MovingToIdle))] // This fires before idle check [EvaluateBefore(typeof(MovingToSliding))] // And before sliding check public struct MovingToJumping : IMyTransition { public bool ShouldTransition(ref MyContext ctx) => ctx.Input.JumpTrigger > 0; } The first transition that returns true wins. Higher-priority transitions should have [EvaluateBefore] pointing to lower-priority ones. 3. Compile and test Enter Play mode and trigger the conditions for your transition. Use the Entity Inspector to watch the state machine component's CurrentState value change. Expected Result When the conditions in ShouldTransition are met, the character exits the source state (OnExitState is called) and enters the target state (OnEnterState is called) at the end of the current frame. Common Mistakes Forgetting [LocomotionTransition] - the source generator will not discover the transition Circular priorities - do not create A [EvaluateBefore] B and B [EvaluateBefore] A Overly broad conditions - a transition that fires too easily can prevent other states from being reached Related Recipe: Add a New State Architecture Overview - How transitions fit into the execution loop

manual/recipes/customize-collision-respo.title: Recipe: Customize Collision Response | MGM Character Controller
manual/recipes/customize-collision-respo.summary: Recipe: Customize Collision Response Add custom behavior when the character hits surfaces - trampolines, breakable walls, damage zones. When to Use You want a state to react to specific surfaces during the collide-and-slide loop without replacing the default collision resolution. Prerequisites A working state machine Familiarity with The Execution Loop (Step 4: Collide-and-Slide) Steps 1. Override OnCollision on the target state OnCollision is called after each collision iteration when a new contact is detected (the contact changed during Move() and the result is not None). It does not replace the default collide-and-slide behavior — it runs after it. public void OnCollision(ref MyContext ctx) { ref readonly var contact = ref ctx.BaseContext.LocomotionContact.ValueRO; // Bounce only on trampoline layer if ((contact.CollisionFilter.BelongsTo & trampolineLayer) == 0) return; if (contact.ContactType == ContactType.Ground) { BaseLocomotionContext baseContext = ctx.BaseContext; baseContext.VerticalVelocity = -baseContext.VerticalVelocity * 1.5f; ctx.BaseContext = baseContext; } } 2. Use contact data for decisions When OnCollision is called, the context contains the updated contact information from the latest Move(): Property Type Description IsInContact bool Any surface contact IsGroundingContact bool Walkable ground (slope below MaxSlope) IsWall bool Surface above MaxSlope IsCeiling bool Surface above the character LocomotionContact.ValueRO.Entity Entity The contacted entity LocomotionContact.ValueRO.Normal float3 Surface normal at contact point LocomotionContact.ValueRO.BodyType BodyType Static, Kinematic, Dynamic, or Character LocomotionContact.ValueRO.CollisionFilter CollisionFilter Collision filter of the contacted body 3. Full override with OnResolveStateCollisions If you need to replace the entire collision resolution step (including Move()), override OnResolveStateCollisions instead. Both hooks are independent — overriding one does not affect the other: public void OnResolveStateCollisions(ref MyContext ctx) { BaseLocomotionContext baseContext = ctx.BaseContext; // Custom collision resolution logic baseContext.Move(); ctx.BaseContext = baseContext; } Note OnResolveStateCollisions is called up to MaxBounce times per frame (once per bounce iteration). OnCollision is only called when a new contact is detected during a bounce. Expected Result The character reacts to specific surfaces with custom physics behavior (bouncing, sliding, stopping) while the standard collide-and-slide handles everything else. Common Mistakes Forgetting the copy-modify-writeback pattern when modifying ctx.BaseContext — changes are lost because BaseContext is a value type Using OnResolveStateCollisions when OnCollision would suffice — the full override is harder to maintain and requires calling Move() yourself Related The Execution Loop - Step 4: Collide-and-Slide Recipe: Push Dynamic Objects

manual/recipes/customize-ground-snapping.title: Recipe: Customize Ground Snapping | MGM Character Controller
manual/recipes/customize-ground-snapping.summary: Recipe: Customize Ground Snapping Override the ground snapping distance to handle slopes, speed, or state-specific behavior. When to Use Characters detach from the ground when walking down slopes or stairs at speed Characters clip through fast-moving descending platforms Prerequisites A working state machine Familiarity with The Execution Loop (Step 5: SnapToGround) How It Works The ILocomotionContext interface provides a default implementation of ComputeGroundSnappingDistance that accounts for step height, slope angle, current slope, and platform velocity. Most projects do not need to override it. If you need custom behavior, implement ComputeGroundSnappingDistance directly on your context struct — the struct method takes precedence over the default interface method: public partial struct MyContext { public void ComputeGroundSnappingDistance( in MyProfile profile, out float distance) { // Custom calculation float stepDistance = CharacterPhysicsProfile.MaxStepHeight; float slopeDistance = math.length(BaseContext.HorizontalVelocity * BaseContext.TimeStep) * math.tan(math.radians(CharacterPhysicsProfile.MaxSlope)); float platformDistance = math.length(BaseContext.PlatformVerticalVelocity) * BaseContext.TimeStep; distance = math.max(stepDistance, slopeDistance) + platformDistance; } } Note The engine already skips ground snapping when the character is moving upward (e.g., during a jump ascent). You do not need to handle that case in your override. Expected Result Characters stay grounded when walking down slopes and stairs. Characters on descending platforms do not detach. Common Mistakes Setting the snap distance too high - the character "vacuums" to the ground from unreasonable heights Forgetting platform velocity - characters lose contact with fast descending platforms Related The Execution Loop - Step 5: SnapToGround Physics Configuration - MaxStepHeight and MaxSlope Moving Platforms - Platform velocity inheritance

manual/recipes/override-pipeline-step.ht.title: Recipe: Override a Pipeline Step | MGM Character Controller
manual/recipes/override-pipeline-step.ht.summary: Recipe: Override a Pipeline Step Replace or wrap a step in the execution loop by overriding a DIM on your state machine struct. When to Use You need custom behavior at the pipeline level - for example, skipping integration when the character is crushed, adding pre/post logic around a step, or replacing ground snapping entirely. Prerequisites A working state machine Familiarity with The Execution Loop and Extending the Package Steps 1. Add a partial struct declaration for your state machine The source generator produces a partial struct for your state machine. Add your own partial file: // File: MyStateMachineExtensions.cs using WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Runtime.StateMachine; namespace MyGame.Locomotion { public partial struct MyStateMachine { // Override goes here } } 2. Override the pipeline method Each execution step is a DIM on ILocomotionStateMachine. Override it by defining the method on your partial struct: Example: Skip integration when crushed public partial struct MyStateMachine { public void Integrate(ref MyStateMachine stateMachine, ref MyContext context) { if (context.BaseContext.OverlapState == OverlapState.Crush) return; // Do nothing when crushed // Otherwise run the default per-state integration stateMachine.IntegratePerState(ref context); } } Example: Skip ground snap above a height threshold public partial struct MyStateMachine { public void SnapToGround(ref MyStateMachine stateMachine, ref MyContext context) { // Skip ground snap when character is above a certain height if (context.BaseContext.CharacterPosition.Position.y > 0) return; } } 3. Available overridable methods Method Calls by default Execute Full pipeline (all steps in order) HandleExternalImpulses Accumulates velocity impulses Integrate IntegratePerState SolveOverlaps Multi-plane de-collision CollideAndSlide ResolveCollisionPerState loop SnapToGround Downward capsule cast InheritDisplacementFromMotionVelocity Platform velocity inheritance EvaluateTransition EvaluateTransitionPerState See Extending the Package for the full reference. Expected Result Your custom logic runs at the correct point in the pipeline, wrapping or replacing the default behavior. Common Mistakes Forgetting to call the default implementation when you only want to add pre/post logic - the step is skipped entirely Overriding IntegratePerState or EvaluateTransitionPerState directly - these are generated dispatchers and not intended to be overridden Related Extending the Package - Full DIM customization reference The Execution Loop - What each step does

manual/recipes/per-character-gravity.htm.title: Recipe: Set Up Per-Character Gravity | MGM Character Controller
manual/recipes/per-character-gravity.htm.summary: Recipe: Set Up Per-Character Gravity Modify gravity per entity for gravity wells, low-gravity zones, or space sections. When to Use You want different characters to experience different gravity - for example, inside a gravity well or on a different planet. Prerequisites A working state machine Familiarity with Physics Configuration Steps 1. Write a system that modifies LocomotionGravity Gravity is stored per-entity in the LocomotionGravity component. Write a system that modifies it: [BurstCompile] [UpdateInGroup(typeof(FixedStepSimulationSystemGroup))] [UpdateBefore(typeof(MyLocomotionSystem))] partial struct GravityZoneSystem : ISystem { [BurstCompile] public void OnUpdate(ref SystemState state) { foreach (var (gravity, transform) in SystemAPI.Query<RefRW<LocomotionGravity>, RefRO<LocalTransform>>()) { float3 pos = transform.ValueRO.Position; // Example: reduce gravity in a zone if (IsInLowGravityZone(pos)) { gravity.ValueRW.Gravity = new float3(0, -4.9f, 0); // Half gravity } else { gravity.ValueRW.Gravity = new float3(0, -9.81f, 0); // Normal } } } } 2. Schedule before the locomotion system Use [UpdateBefore(typeof(MyLocomotionSystem))] so the gravity is set before the locomotion job reads it. 3. Gravity direction changes For non-downward gravity (e.g., walking on walls or ceilings), set the gravity direction: gravity.ValueRW.Gravity = new float3(1, 0, 0) * 9.81f; // Gravity pulls to the right The BaseLocomotionContext decomposes this into GravityDirection and GravityStrength, which your state logic uses for integration. Expected Result Characters in the gravity zone fall slower (or in a different direction). The state machine and collide-and-slide algorithm adapt automatically because they read GravityDirection and GravityStrength from the context. Common Mistakes Scheduling after the locomotion system - the old gravity value is used for the current frame Modifying GravityDirection directly on the context inside a state - this is overwritten each frame from the component. Modify the LocomotionGravity component instead. Related Physics Configuration - LocomotionGravity section Moving Platforms - Gravity wells in the sample

manual/recipes/push-dynamic-objects.html.title: Recipe: Push Dynamic Objects on Collision | MGM Character Controller
manual/recipes/push-dynamic-objects.html.summary: Recipe: Push Dynamic Objects on Collision Apply velocity impulses to dynamic rigid bodies when the character collides with them. When to Use You want the character to push crates, barrels, or other dynamic physics objects when walking into them. Prerequisites A working state machine Dynamic rigid bodies in your scene with PhysicsBody (set to Dynamic) Steps 1. Override OnCollision In the states where you want push behavior (typically MovingState), add push logic in OnCollision: public void OnCollision(ref MyContext ctx) { ref readonly var contact = ref ctx.BaseContext.LocomotionContact.ValueRO; if (contact.BodyType != BodyType.Dynamic) return; BaseLocomotionContext baseContext = ctx.BaseContext; float3 pushVelocity = math.normalizesafe(baseContext.HorizontalVelocity) * math.length(baseContext.HorizontalVelocity) * 0.5f; // Apply impulse to the dynamic body baseContext.ApplyImpulse( ref baseContext, contact.BodyIndex, contact.Entity, baseContext.PhysicsWorld.GetRigidBodyIndex(baseContext.CharacterEntity), baseContext.CharacterEntity, pushVelocity); ctx.BaseContext = baseContext; } OnCollision is called when a new contact is detected, which means the impulse is applied once per contact — not every bounce iteration. 2. Tune the push force Adjust the multiplier (0.5f above) based on your game's feel. Higher values push objects further. The character's Mass and MassToDynamic on the physics profile also affect the default impulse behavior from HandleExternalImpulses. Expected Result When the character walks into a dynamic body, the body is pushed in the direction the character is moving. The push force scales with the character's speed. Common Mistakes Using the copy-modify-writeback pattern but forgetting ctx.BaseContext = baseContext at the end - the impulse is lost Applying impulse in OnIntegrateState instead of a collision hook - contacts are not available yet during integration Related The Execution Loop - Step 4: Collide-and-Slide Physics Configuration - Mass and MassToDynamic Recipe: Customize Collision Response

manual/recipes/run-multiple-state-machin.title: Recipe: Run Multiple State Machines | MGM Character Controller
manual/recipes/run-multiple-state-machin.summary: Recipe: Run Multiple State Machines Use different state machine types for different character archetypes in the same project. When to Use Your game has fundamentally different movement types - for example, ground characters and vehicles - that should not share a state enum. Prerequisites One working state machine already created Familiarity with Creating a State Machine Steps 1. Create a second state machine Use the scaffolding tool again: Right-click in a different folder in the Project window Select Assets > Create > WAYN Games > MGM Character Controller > StateMachine Name it (e.g., Vehicle) This generates a completely independent set of types: VehicleStateMachine, VehicleStates, VehicleContext, VehicleProfile, VehicleInput, VehicleStateData, and all authoring/editor files. 2. Define states and transitions for the new machine Each state machine has its own enum, states, transitions, profile, and input. They are fully independent - no shared types needed. 3. Create entities with the new machine Use the generated VehicleCharacterLocomotorAuthoring and VehiclePlayerCharacterAuthoring components on new entities. Each state machine type generates its own authoring components and systems. 4. Both run simultaneously The source generator creates a separate ISystem for each state machine type. Both systems schedule their own LocomotionJob independently, processing their respective entities in parallel. Expected Result Ground characters and vehicles coexist in the same scene, each with their own state machine, states, transitions, and physics behavior. They share the same BaseLocomotionContext and collide-and-slide infrastructure. Switching an entity between state machines This is an advanced pattern. To switch a character from one state machine to another (e.g., entering a vehicle), you need to: Remove the components of the old state machine (state machine, input, profile, state data) Add the components of the new state machine This involves structural entity changes, which should happen via an EntityCommandBuffer. Common Mistakes Trying to share states between state machines - states are bound to a specific state machine via [LocomotionState(typeof(...))] Putting both state machine's components on the same entity - each entity should have exactly one state machine type Related Creating a State Machine - Scaffolding tool Extending the Package - Architecture details

manual/recipes/setup-hybrid-character.ht.title: Recipe: Set Up a Hybrid Character | MGM Character Controller
manual/recipes/setup-hybrid-character.ht.summary: Recipe: Set Up a Hybrid Character Create a character with both ECS locomotion and a visual GameObject model. When to Use You want a character driven by the Burst-compiled locomotion job that also has a visible 3D model with an Animator, particle effects, audio, or other MonoBehaviour-based features. Prerequisites A working state machine created with the scaffolding tool or manually Familiarity with Hybrid GameObject Layer concepts Steps 1. Create the Locomotor entity (in sub-scene) Add your Character Locomotor Authoring component as described in Getting Started. 2. Create the Model (in sub-scene) Add a child GameObject under (or near) the Locomotor with your visual mesh/model Add a CharacterFollowerAuthoring component Set the Locomotor field to reference the Locomotor's authoring component The model can have an Animator, AudioSource, particle systems, etc. 3. Create the Player prefab (regular GameObject, NOT in sub-scene) Create a GameObject with Camera, CinemachineBrain, PlayerInput Add a PlayerGoAuthoring component with camera references Save it as a prefab 4. Create the Player entity (in sub-scene) Create a GameObject in the sub-scene Add your Player Character Authoring component Assign the character entity, input asset, and player GO prefab What Happens at Runtime The Player entity is baked, triggering PlayerGoManagerSystem to instantiate the player prefab The input asset is initialized with the player's camera and input references The model entity follows the Locomotor via CharacterFollowerSystem The model's GameObject transform is synced via EntityGoTransformSyncSystem Next Steps Integration: Animation - Connecting an Animator to a hybrid character Integration: Cinemachine - Camera setup on player GameObjects

manual/recipes/setup-moving-platform.htm.title: Recipe: Set Up a Moving Platform | MGM Character Controller
manual/recipes/setup-moving-platform.htm.summary: Recipe: Set Up a Moving Platform Create a moving or rotating platform that characters can ride. When to Use You want a kinematic body that moves along a track or rotates, and characters standing on it should automatically inherit its velocity and rotation. Prerequisites A sub-scene with physics enabled Familiarity with Moving Platforms concepts Steps 1. Create the platform GameObject (in sub-scene) Create a GameObject with your platform mesh Add a physics collider (Box, Mesh, etc.) Set the collider as Kinematic (not Dynamic, not Static) 2. Add platform authoring Add a PlatformAuthoring component. 3. Add movement behavior Add a MovingPlatformAuthoring component and configure: Speed: Movement speed along the track in world units per second Rotation: Rotation axis and speed (float3) Track (optional): Define waypoints for the platform to follow 4. Collision layers Ensure the platform's collision layer is included in your character's CollidesWith mask. By default, both use all layers, so this works out of the box. Next Steps Moving Platforms - How platform inheritance works Physics Configuration - Collision layer setup

manual/recipes/switch-to-ai-input.html.title: Recipe: Switch a Character to AI Input | MGM Character Controller
manual/recipes/switch-to-ai-input.html.summary: Recipe: Switch a Character to AI Input Drive a character with an AI system instead of player input. When to Use You want NPC characters that use the same state machine and movement logic as players, but are controlled by AI decision-making instead of hardware input. Prerequisites A working character with a state machine Familiarity with Input System Steps 1. Create a tag component public struct AIControlled : IComponentData { } Add this to AI-controlled character entities during baking (via an authoring component or a system). 2.
