Create an AI input system Write a system that writes directly to the input ECS component, bypassing PlayerInputAsset: [BurstCompile] [UpdateInGroup(typeof(SimulationSystemGroup))] partial struct AIInputSystem : ISystem { [BurstCompile] public void OnUpdate(ref SystemState state) { foreach (var (input, transform) in SystemAPI.Query<RefRW<MyInput>, RefRO<LocalTransform>>() .WithAll<AIControlled>()) { // Example: move toward a target position float3 targetDir = math.normalizesafe( GetTargetPosition() - transform.ValueRO.Position); input.ValueRW.Movement = targetDir; input.ValueRW.JumpTrigger = ShouldJump() ? 1f : 0f; } } } 3. Skip the Player entity for AI characters AI characters do not need a Player entity, player prefab, or PlayerInputAsset. They only need the Locomotor entity with the state machine components and the AIControlled tag. During baking, add the input component directly without the player input pipeline: public class AICharacterAuthoring : MonoBehaviour { // Baking adds MyInput component + AIControlled tag } 4. (Optional) Shared state machine The AI character uses the exact same state machine, states, transitions, profile, and physics as player characters. No code changes needed in the state machine - it only reads the TInput component, which is written by your AI system instead of the player input pipeline. Expected Result AI characters move, jump, and transition between states identically to player characters. The only difference is the source of input data. Common Mistakes Forgetting to add the input component to the AI entity - the locomotion system will not process it Writing input every frame without accumulating triggers - jump triggers can be missed between fixed updates (use the same accumulation pattern as player input) Related Input System - Full input architecture Architecture Overview - Entity types and roles

manual/samples.html.title: Samples | MGM Character Controller
manual/samples.html.summary: Samples This page lists all samples included with the MGM Character Controller and provides a recommended study order. SinglePlayerSample Teaches Complete state machine with five states, transitions, input handling, camera setup, and profile configuration Difficulty Beginner to intermediate Setup time 5 minutes (see Quickstart) Dependencies Unity Input System, Cinemachine What It Contains The sample provides a full reference implementation of a third-person character controller: 5 states: Idle, Moving, Jumping, Falling, Sliding 18 transitions: All valid state-to-state transitions with priority ordering Input handling: Camera-relative movement with jump trigger accumulation Profile: Tunable speed, acceleration, jump force, gravity, and terminal velocity Prefabs: Character (Locomotor + Model) and Player (Camera + Input) Assets: Pre-configured profile and input ScriptableObjects Key Files - Study Order Read the sample code in this order to build the mental model: Order File What It Teaches 1 Runtime/LocomotionStateMachine.cs The anchor struct - [LocomotionStateMachine] attribute triggers source generation 2 Runtime/Data/LocomotionInput.cs Input struct with continuous (Movement) and trigger (JumpTrigger) fields, plus accumulation in ApplyInputsToCharacter 3 Runtime/Data/LocomotionProfile.cs Profile struct composed of sub-profiles (MovingProfile, FallingProfile, JumpingProfile) 4 Runtime/Data/LocomotionStateData.cs Per-state persistent data (JumpData with jump count) 5 Runtime/States/IdleState.cs Simplest state - zeros velocity on enter, handles platform rotation 6 Runtime/States/MovingState.cs Horizontal acceleration from input, character rotation toward movement direction 7 Runtime/States/FallingState.cs Gravity application with terminal velocity, reuses MovingState's horizontal acceleration 8 Runtime/States/JumpingState.cs Jump force application, trigger consumption, multi-jump support via StateData 9 Runtime/States/SlidingState.cs Gravity-driven sliding on steep slopes 10 Runtime/Transitions/LocomotionTransitions.cs All transitions as small structs with [LocomotionTransition] and [EvaluateBefore] for priority ordering 11 Runtime/Data/LocomotionContextExtensions.cs Custom ground snapping distance that accounts for slope angle and platform velocity 12 Runtime/Data/LocomotionPlayerInputAsset.cs Camera-relative input reading and Cinemachine setup Things to Try After running the sample, try these modifications to deepen your understanding: Change movement speed - Open the SinglePlayerProfileAsset and modify Moving > MaxSpeed. Notice how the profile is baked, so you need to re-enter Play mode. Enable double jump - In the profile, set Jumping > MaxJumps to 2. The JumpingState already supports multi-jump via StateData.JumpData.JumpsCount. Disable air control - In JumpingState.OnIntegrateState, remove the horizontal acceleration block. The character will maintain its launch velocity with no air steering. Add debug visualization - Select the character entity in the sub-scene and add a LocomotionDebugConfigAuthoring component. Enter Play mode and observe contact points and movement paths in the Scene view. Related Docs Quickstart - Set up and run this sample Writing Custom States - How the state lifecycle works Creating a State Machine - How the scaffolding tool generates this structure Input System - Input architecture in depth FlyCamSample Teaches Minimal single-state fly camera, gravity-free movement, split entity/camera rotation, collision handling without ground contact Difficulty Beginner Setup time 5 minutes Dependencies Unity Input System What It Contains A first-person fly camera controller for navigating test scenes. The camera floats freely without gravity, slides along obstacles, and uses mouse look for rotation. 1 state: Flying (always active — no transitions needed) No gravity: Zero vertical acceleration, ground snapping disabled Split rotation: Entity rotates in yaw only; pitch is applied to the camera GameObject Collision: Slides along walls and ceilings via the default collide-and-slide pipeline Input: WASD/ZQSD movement, mouse look, Space to fly up, Left Ctrl to fly down Controls Input Action W / Z Move forward S Move backward A / Q Strafe left D Strafe right Mouse Look (yaw + pitch) Space Fly up Left Ctrl Fly down Movement follows the camera look direction — looking down and pressing forward moves the camera downward toward where it is looking. How It Works Architecture The FlyCam follows the same Player → Character entity split as the SinglePlayerSample but is intentionally minimal: classDiagram direction LR class PlayerEntity["Player Entity"] { CharacterFollower LocomotionInputAsset PlayerGameObjectPrefab } class CharacterEntity["Character Entity"] { FlyCamStateMachine FlyCamInput FlyCamStateData ~yaw~ FlyCamProfile LocomotionVelocity LocomotionContact PhysicsCollider ~capsule~ } class PlayerGo["PlayerGo (GameObject)"] { PlayerGoAuthoring PlayerInput } class CameraTarget["CameraTarget (Transform)"] { Camera } PlayerEntity --> CharacterEntity : CharacterFollower PlayerEntity ..> PlayerGo : instantiates at runtime PlayerGo --> CameraTarget : child transform Rotation: Entity vs Camera The entity only rotates around the gravity axis (yaw). This is necessary because the locomotion pipeline applies gravity alignment before the state runs, which would fight with any pitch applied to the entity. Pitch is handled entirely on the managed (GameObject) side: FlyCamPlayerInputAsset.ReadInputsFromAsset accumulates pitch from mouse Y delta It applies pitch as localRotation on the Camera transform It passes the pitch angle to the ECS input struct so the state can compute a pitched movement direction // In FlyCamPlayerInputAsset — managed side m_Pitch = Mathf.Clamp(m_Pitch - look.y * LookSensitivity, -MaxPitch, MaxPitch); m_CameraTransform.localRotation = Quaternion.Euler(m_Pitch, 0f, 0f); inputs.Pitch = m_Pitch; // In FlyCamFlyingState — ECS side (yaw only on entity) quaternion rotation = quaternion.AxisAngle(up, math.radians(stateData.Yaw)); baseContext.CharacterPosition.Rotation = rotation; // Pitched forward for movement direction quaternion pitchedRotation = math.mul(rotation, quaternion.AxisAngle(math.right(), math.radians(ctx.Input.Pitch))); float3 forward = math.mul(pitchedRotation, math.forward()); Movement Without Gravity The state computes desired velocity from input and uses MathematicsHelpers.ComputeAccelerationToTargetVelocity for smooth acceleration/deceleration. No gravity acceleration is applied — both horizontal and vertical channels are purely input-driven. The desired direction is split into horizontal and vertical components relative to the gravity axis, then passed to UpdateVelocitiesAndComputeDisplacements with zero gravity contribution. Ground Snapping Disabled The FlyCamContext partial overrides ComputeGroundSnappingDistance to return zero, preventing the snap-to-ground step from pulling the camera to the floor: public partial struct FlyCamContext { public void ComputeGroundSnappingDistance(in FlyCamProfile profile, out float distance) { distance = 0f; } } Stale Contact Clearing A flying entity has no transitions to clear the contact state (unlike a grounded character that transitions to Falling). Without clearing, a contact from a previous collision would persist and cause Move() to project displacement incorrectly on subsequent frames. The state clears the contact at the start of each integration: baseContext.LocomotionContact.ValueRW = LocomotionContact.None; The collide-and-slide step will set a fresh contact if a collision occurs that frame. Scene Setup [Screenshot placeholder: Scene hierarchy showing Player and Character entities in the subscene] [Screenshot placeholder: Character entity inspector with FlyCamCharacterLocomotionAuthoring and physics profile] [Screenshot placeholder: PlayerGo prefab showing PlayerGoAuthoring, PlayerInput, CameraTarget with child Camera] [Screenshot placeholder: FlyCamPlayerInputAsset inspector with Move, Look, FlyUp, FlyDown action references assigned] The sample scene contains: Subscene with: A character entity (capsule collider) with FlyCamCharacterLocomotionAuthoring — assigns the profile asset and configures the physics capsule A player entity with FlyCamPlayerCharacterAuthoring — references the character, input asset, and PlayerGo prefab PlayerGo prefab (instantiated at runtime): PlayerGoAuthoring component with Camera and CameraTarget references PlayerInput component bound to FlyCamActionMap A CameraTarget child Transform with the Camera as its child Important The Camera must be a child of the CameraTarget Transform. The CameraRigFollowControlledCharacterSystem moves the CameraTarget to match the character entity's position. The Camera inherits that position and adds its own local pitch rotation. Key Files - Study Order Order File What It Teaches 1 Runtime/FlyCamStateMachine.cs Minimal state machine anchor — single [LocomotionStateMachine] attribute 2 Runtime/Data/FlyCamInput.cs Input with continuous (Movement, Pitch), accumulated (LookDelta +=), and overwritten (VerticalMovement) fields 3 Runtime/Data/FlyCamProfile.cs Simple profile with just movement tuning and look sensitivity 4 Runtime/Data/FlyCamStateData.cs Minimal state data — only yaw angle 5 Runtime/Data/FlyCamContextExtensions.cs Overriding a DIM default to disable ground snapping 6 Runtime/States/FlyCamFlyingState.cs Core logic: split yaw/pitch rotation, pitched movement direction, zero-gravity movement, stale contact clearing 7 Runtime/Data/FlyCamPlayerInputAsset.cs Managed-side pitch handling, camera rotation, Input System action reading Things to Try Adjust fly speed - Open the FlyCamProfileAsset and change Moving > MaxSpeed. Higher values make the camera faster. TimeToMaxSpeed and TimeToStop control acceleration and deceleration feel. Change mouse sensitivity - Modify LookSensitivity on either the FlyCamProfileAsset (affects yaw) or the FlyCamPlayerInputAsset (affects pitch). Disable collision - Either set the physics profile's collision filter to collide with nothing in the FlyCamCharacterLocomotionAuthoring inspector, or override OnResolveStateCollisions in FlyCamFlyingState to move the locomotor directly without any collision check. Both approaches help understand what the collide-and-slide pipeline does. Add a speed boost - Create a second profile with higher MaxSpeed and swap profiles at runtime, or add a sprint input to the action map. Related Docs Creating a State Machine - Full scaffolding walkthrough Writing Custom States - State lifecycle and DIM overrides Input System - Player → Character input pipeline Customize Ground Snapping - More on the ComputeGroundSnappingDistance override Customize Collision Response - Overriding OnResolveStateCollisions

manual/troubleshooting.html.title: Troubleshooting | MGM Character Controller
manual/troubleshooting.html.summary: Troubleshooting This page lists common issues organized by symptom. For each issue: what you see, why it happens, and how to fix it. Character falls through the floor Cause: The floor does not have a physics collider that the ECS world can see. Fix: Select the floor GameObject inside the sub-scene Add a collider component to the floor Verify the sub-scene is loaded (check Hierarchy for the sub-scene icon) Verify: Enter Play mode. Open the Physics Debugger (Window > Analysis > Physics Debugger) and confirm the floor collider is visible. Prevention: Always ensure environment geometry inside sub-scenes has a collider. Character doesn't respond to input Cause: Input is not flowing from the hardware to the ECS component. Fix - check each layer in order: Input System package: Confirm com.unity.inputsystem is installed and the Active Input Handling is set to Input System Package (New) or Both in Edit > Project Settings > Player Player entity: Verify the Player entity has the input asset assigned in the Player Character Authoring component Player prefab: The Player GO Prefab must have a PlayerInput component with input actions assigned Character reference: The Player entity must reference the correct Character entity Verify: Add a breakpoint or log in your PlayerInputAsset.ReadInputsFromAsset() method to confirm it is called each frame. Character jitters on the ground Cause: The character's visual position updates discretely each fixed step, causing visible stuttering. Fix: Set InterpolationMode to Interpolated in the Character Physics Profile If still jittering, increase CollisionTolerance slightly (try 0.01) Verify the ground collider is static (not a dynamic body rebuilding each frame) Verify: The jitter should disappear or become imperceptible at 60+ FPS. Character walks through walls Cause: The collide-and-slide algorithm does not have enough iterations to resolve the collision. Fix: Increase MaxBounce on the Character Physics Profile (try 5-6, default is 3) Check that the wall's collision layer is included in the character's CollidesWith mask If the character is moving very fast, the displacement per frame may exceed the collider size - reduce speed or increase the fixed update rate Verify: Enable debug visualization with contact points to see if wall contacts are being detected. Character cannot climb steps Cause: Step detection conditions are not met. Fix: Increase MaxStepHeight on the Character Physics Profile Ensure MaxSlope is high enough that the step's vertical face is classified as a wall (not ground) Verify the step geometry is clean - sloped mesh artifacts can confuse the step detector Verify: Enable debug visualization. Wall contacts should appear at the step face, followed by successful upward movement. Character slides off walkable slopes Cause: The slope angle exceeds the MaxSlope threshold. Fix: Increase MaxSlope on the Character Physics Profile. The default handles most cases at 45-60 degrees. Verify: Log or inspect ctx.BaseContext.LocomotionContact.ValueRO.Normal to compute the actual slope angle. Jump doesn't fire Cause: The jump trigger input is being overwritten instead of accumulated, or it is not being consumed after use. Fix: In your ICharacterLocomotionInput.ApplyInputsToCharacter, accumulate the trigger: JumpTrigger += playerInputs.JumpTrigger (not JumpTrigger = playerInputs.JumpTrigger) In your PlayerInputAsset.ReadInputsFromAsset, return 1f for the trigger only on the frame the button was pressed (WasPerformedThisFrame()), not while it is held Verify: Add a temporary log in ApplyInputsToCharacter to see the accumulated value. Jump fires every frame Cause: The jump trigger is not being consumed (reset to zero) after use. Fix: After reading the trigger value in your state's OnIntegrateState, reset it: LocomotionInput value = ctx.Input; value.JumpTrigger = 0; ctx.Input = value; Verify: The character should jump once per button press, not continuously. State transition never fires Cause: The transition's conditions are not met, or a higher-priority transition is firing first. Fix: Check the [EvaluateBefore] attributes on your transitions - they control evaluation order. The first matching transition wins. Verify the conditions in your ShouldTransition method match the actual runtime state. Use the Entity Inspector to check LocomotionContact, LocomotionVelocity, and your input component values. If using the inline EvaluateTransitions pattern, check the order of if checks - earlier checks have higher priority. Verify: Add a temporary log inside the transition's ShouldTransition to see which condition fails. Character launched unexpectedly Cause: An external impulse is applied from a dynamic body collision, or platform velocity is being applied twice. Fix: Enable impulse debug visualization to see where forces are coming from Check that you are not manually adding PlatformHorizontalVelocity in OnIntegrateState - the system handles platform inheritance automatically in the ComputeInheritedDisplacement step If dynamic body collisions are injecting too much velocity, check the Mass and MassToDynamic values on the Character Physics Profile Verify: Disable impulse handling temporarily by overriding HandleExternalImpulses to a no-op on your state machine struct. Character clips through or gets stuck on moving platforms Cause: The ground snapping distance is too small for the platform's speed. Fix: The default ComputeGroundSnappingDistance on ILocomotionContext already accounts for platform vertical velocity. If you have overridden it on your context struct, ensure your implementation includes platform velocity in the distance calculation. See Recipe: Customize Ground Snapping for details. Verify: Enable path point debug visualization to see if the character loses ground contact on fast platforms. Source generator not producing code Cause: The source generator cannot find or process your state machine attributes. Fix: Ensure your state enum has the [LocomotionStateMachine] attribute Ensure each enum value has a [LocomotionState(typeof(YourState))] attribute Check that your project references the WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Runtime assembly Restart Unity or reimport the package - the source generator runs at compile time Verify: Check the Roslyn Analyzers section in your assembly definition to confirm the source generator DLL is referenced. Build compiles but character doesn't work in player Cause: Generic types used by the source generator may not be registered for AOT compilation. Fix: Ensure you have [assembly: RegisterGenericComponentType(typeof(LocomotionInputAsset<YourInput>))] in your assembly Check for Burst compilation errors in the build log - state methods must be Burst-compatible (no managed types) Verify there are no managed types (string, class, arrays) in state structs or context Verify: Build with Development Build enabled and check the Player log for Burst or ECS errors. Debug visualization not showing Cause: Debug components are not added or the Scene view is not visible. Fix: Select the character entity's authoring GameObject in the sub-scene Add a LocomotionDebugConfigAuthoring component Configure which visualizations to enable in the Inspector Enter Play mode and switch to the Scene view (debug gizmos do not render in Game view) Verify: Debug data is baked into a blob asset, so changes require re-entering Play mode. Next Steps Debug Visualization - Detailed guide on the debug system Physics Configuration - Tuning physical parameters Getting Started - Verify your setup is correct

manual/upgrade-guide.html.title: Upgrade Guide | MGM Character Controller
manual/upgrade-guide.html.summary: Upgrade Guide This page documents breaking changes and migration steps between versions. 0.4.x to 0.5.0 Release date: 2026-03-24 Breaking Changes Package renamed The package identifier changed from com.wayngamesassets.mgm.charactercontroller to wayn.games.mgm.character-controller. Migration: In your Unity project's Packages/manifest.json, update the dependency name: // Before "com.wayngamesassets.mgm.charactercontroller": "..." // After "wayn.games.mgm.character-controller": "..." Namespace renamed All namespaces changed from WAYN.Games.MGM.Locomotion.* to WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.*. Old Namespace New Namespace WAYN.Games.MGM.Locomotion.Runtime.StateMachine WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Runtime.StateMachine WAYN.Games.MGM.Locomotion.Runtime.Components WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Runtime.Components WAYN.Games.MGM.Locomotion.Runtime.Physics WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Runtime.Physics WAYN.Games.MGM.Locomotion.Authoring WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Authoring WAYN.Games.MGM.Locomotion.Hybrid.* WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Hybrid.* Migration: Find-and-replace in all your .cs files: WAYN.Games.MGM.Locomotion → WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController Assembly definitions renamed All assembly names changed. If you reference them by string (not GUID), update the references: Old Assembly Name New Assembly Name Wayn.Games.Locomotion.Runtime WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Runtime Wayn.Games.Locomotion.Editor WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Editor Wayn.Games.Locomotion.Hybrid WAYN.Games.MGM.CharacterController.Hybrid Migration: If your .asmdef files reference the package assemblies by GUID, no changes are needed. If they reference by name string, update to the new names. Editor menu path changed The Unity editor menu moved from Assets > Create > WAYN Games > Locomotion to Assets > Create > WAYN Games > MGM Character Controller. How to Verify the Upgrade Update manifest.json with the new package name Find-and-replace the old namespace in all your scripts Compile the project — fix any remaining type references Enter Play mode and verify character movement works as before 0.3.0 to 0.4.0 Release date: 2026-03-22 Breaking Changes Source generator rewrite The source generator was rewritten. ILocomotionStateMachineDefinition has been removed and replaced by attribute-based discovery. Before (0.3.0): public struct MyStateMachineDefinition : ILocomotionStateMachineDefinition { ... } After (0.4.0): [LocomotionStateMachine] public partial struct MyStateMachine { } Migration: Delete your old definition struct. Create a new struct with the [LocomotionStateMachine] attribute. The source generator discovers states and transitions from [LocomotionState] and [LocomotionTransition] attributes. DIM lifecycle defaults The source generator now emits explicit struct forwarding methods for default interface method lifecycle hooks. If you were relying on the DIM defaults being called implicitly, this should work the same. If you had overridden the DIM on a partial struct, verify your overrides still compile. Editor menu reorganization Creation menus, script templates, and helpers moved into Editor/CreationMenus/ subfolder. If you had scripts referencing the old paths, update the references. Locomotion script scaffolding New CreateLocomotionScript menu and LocomotionScriptCreationMenus replace the old scaffolding. The generated folder structure and file naming have changed: All scaffolded files are now prefixed with the state machine name to avoid collisions across multiple state machines Assembly definitions are auto-generated with proper namespaces and GUID references Migration: If you used the old scaffolding tool, the generated files still work. New files generated by the updated tool will use the new naming convention. How to Verify the Upgrade Open the project in Unity 6000.3+ Wait for compilation to complete Check the Console for errors - fix any missing type references Enter Play mode with an existing scene Verify character movement works as before 0.2.0 to 0.3.0 Release date: 2026-03-18 Breaking Changes ILocomotionState uses instance-method DIM pattern State lifecycle methods changed from internal static to instance methods with default interface implementations. Before (0.2.0): internal static void OnEnterState(ref MyContext ctx) { } After (0.3.0): public void OnEnterState(ref MyContext ctx) { } Migration: Change all state lifecycle methods from internal static to public instance methods. CharacterPhysicsProfile moved to ILocomotionContext CharacterPhysicsProfile is no longer part of ILocomotionProfile. It is now a property on ILocomotionContext. Before: ctx.Profile.PhysicsProfileValue After: ctx.CharacterPhysicsProfile Migration: Move physics profile access from ctx.Profile to ctx.CharacterPhysicsProfile. Remove PhysicsProfileValue from your profile struct. Base authoring classes renamed Old Name New Name BaseCharacterAuthoring BaseCharacterLocomotorAuthoring BaseCharacterBaker BaseCharacterLocomotorBaker Migration: Find-and-replace the old names in your authoring scripts. Platform velocity inheritance The path-tracking platform system was replaced with a MotionVelocity-based approach via ComputeInheritedDisplacement. Migration: If you were using the old PlatformPath components, remove them. Platform inheritance now works automatically for any dynamic body the character is grounded on. Access platform data via ctx.BaseContext.PlatformHorizontalVelocity and ctx.BaseContext.PlatformVerticalVelocity. Transition evaluation timing EvaluateTransitions now runs once at the end of the execution loop (after all physics), not after each bounce iteration. Migration: If your transitions depend on mid-frame contact data, adapt them to use the final post-physics contact state instead. How to Verify Compile the project - fix renamed types and changed method signatures Enter Play mode and test all states and transitions Test moving platforms - verify characters ride them correctly Enable debug visualization to confirm contact detection still works 0.1.0 to 0.2.0 Release date: 2025-10-05 Changes Added source generator for state machine systems Added state machine creation menu Refactored systems to support user-defined state machines Migration: The 0.1.0 API was internal/preview. Recreate your state machine using the new creation menu (Assets > Create > WAYN Games > MGM Character Controller > StateMachine). Next Steps Getting Started - Set up a character with the current API Creating a State Machine - Current state machine structure

manual/writing-states.html.title: Writing Custom States | MGM Character Controller
manual/writing-states.html.summary: Writing Custom States States are the building blocks of character behavior. Each state defines how the character moves, reacts to physics, and transitions to other states. This guide covers the state interface, lifecycle methods, and common patterns. The State Interface Every state is an unmanaged struct that implements ILocomotionState: [LocomotionState(typeof(MyCharacterStateMachine))] internal partial struct MyState { public void OnEnterState(ref MyContext ctx) { } public void OnIntegrateState(ref MyContext ctx) { } public void OnResolveStateCollisions(ref MyContext ctx) { } public void OnExitState(ref MyContext ctx) { } } Key rules: All lifecycle methods are instance methods with default interface implementations (DIM pattern). They are public void, not internal static. The ILocomotionState interface is added by the source generator via the partial mechanism. Transitions are handled by separate transition structs (see Transitions below) - the state struct does not define transition logic The source generator dispatches calls to the correct state struct - no virtual dispatch at runtime State methods run inside an already Burst-compiled job, so explicit [BurstCompile] on state structs and methods is not required No managed types (strings, classes, arrays) - this code runs inside Burst jobs Lifecycle Methods OnEnterState Called once when the state machine transitions into this state. Use it to: Initialize state-specific data (reset timers, counters) Set initial velocities Clear contact information (for airborne states) public void OnEnterState(ref MyContext ctx) { // Entering a grounded state: cancel all vertical momentum ctx.BaseContext.VerticalVelocity = float3.zero; ctx.BaseContext.VerticalDisplacement = float3.zero; } OnIntegrateState Called once per frame during the integration phase. This is where you compute the character's desired displacement based on input, gravity, and profile parameters. Your job here is to populate HorizontalDisplacement, VerticalDisplacement, HorizontalVelocity, and VerticalVelocity on the BaseLocomotionContext. The easiest way is to compute accelerations and call UpdateVelocitiesAndComputeDisplacements: public void OnIntegrateState(ref MyContext ctx) { ref BaseLocomotionContext baseContext = ref ctx.BaseContext; // Compute gravity float3 gravityAcceleration = baseContext.GravityDirection * baseContext.GravityStrength; // Compute movement acceleration from input float3 desiredVelocity = ctx.Input.MovementDirection * ctx.Profile.MaxSpeed; float accel = ctx.Profile.MaxSpeed / ctx.Profile.TimeToMaxSpeed; MathematicsHelpers.ComputeAccelerationToTargetVelocity( out float3 moveAcceleration, baseContext.HorizontalVelocity, desiredVelocity, baseContext.TimeStep, accel); // Apply both accelerations baseContext.UpdateVelocitiesAndComputeDisplacements( newHorizontalAcceleration: moveAcceleration, newVerticalAcceleration: gravityAcceleration); } Important Do not add platform velocity here. The ComputeInheritedDisplacement step handles it automatically after collision resolution. Adding it manually would cause double-application. The Context Copy Pattern Because BaseLocomotionContext is a value type on the context interface, modifying it requires either: Option A: Use a ref local (when your context exposes the backing field): ref BaseLocomotionContext baseContext = ref ctx.BaseContext; baseContext.HorizontalVelocity = float3.zero; // Changes are written through the ref Option B: Copy, modify, write back (when going through the interface): BaseLocomotionContext baseContext = ctx.BaseContext; baseContext.HorizontalVelocity = float3.zero; ctx.BaseContext = baseContext; The sample uses ref access because the context struct exposes BaseContext as a public field in addition to the interface property. OnResolveStateCollisions Called potentially multiple times per frame (up to MaxBounce iterations). Each call represents one iteration of the collide-and-slide algorithm. In most cases, your implementation simply delegates to baseContext.Move(): public void OnResolveStateCollisions(ref MyContext ctx) { BaseLocomotionContext baseContext = ctx.BaseContext; baseContext.Move(); ctx.BaseContext = baseContext; } Note You must use the copy-modify-writeback pattern here because Move() modifies the context's position, displacement, and contact data internally. For advanced use cases, you can add custom collision response logic. For example, applying an impulse to dynamic bodies the character hits: public void OnResolveStateCollisions(ref MyContext ctx) { BaseLocomotionContext baseContext = ctx.BaseContext; baseContext.Move(); // Push dynamic bodies away on contact if (baseContext.IsInContact && baseContext.LocomotionContact.ValueRO.BodyType == BodyType.Dynamic) { float3 pushVelocity = baseContext.HorizontalVelocity * 0.5f; baseContext.ApplyImpulse( ref baseContext, baseContext.LocomotionContact.ValueRO.BodyIndex, baseContext.LocomotionContact.ValueRO.Entity, /* impactorBodyIndex */ ..., baseContext.CharacterEntity, pushVelocity); } ctx.BaseContext = baseContext; } OnExitState Called once when the state machine transitions out of this state. Use it to clean up state-specific data: public void OnExitState(ref MyContext ctx) { // Reset jump counter when leaving the jumping state ctx.StateData.JumpData.JumpsCount = 0; } Transitions Transitions define when and how the character moves between states. The package supports two patterns. Recommended: Attribute-Based Transitions Declare transitions as small structs with [LocomotionTransition]. The source generator discovers them and handles dispatch, OnExitState, and OnEnterState automatically. [LocomotionTransition(typeof(IdleState), typeof(JumpingState))] [EvaluateBefore(typeof(IdleToMoving))] [EvaluateBefore(typeof(IdleToFalling))] public struct IdleToJumping : IMyTransition { public bool ShouldTransition(ref MyContext ctx) => ctx.BaseContext.IsGroundingContact && ctx.Input.JumpTrigger > 0; } Key rules: The struct name follows the {Source}To{Target} convention [LocomotionTransition(typeof(FromState), typeof(ToState))] declares which states are connected ShouldTransition returns true when the transition should fire [EvaluateBefore] / [EvaluateAfter] control priority - the first matching transition wins The source generator calls OnExitState on the source and OnEnterState on the target automatically See the sample's LocomotionTransitions.cs for a complete example with 17 transitions. The attribute-based pattern is the only supported approach. The source generator discovers all [LocomotionTransition] structs, handles exit/enter calls, and makes transition priority explicit via [EvaluateBefore]/[EvaluateAfter] attributes. Consuming Trigger Input Trigger inputs like jump presses must be consumed after use. Otherwise, the trigger fires every frame: static void ConsumeJumpTrigger(ref MyContext ctx) { MyInput value = ctx.Input; value.JumpTrigger = 0; ctx.Input = value; } Call this in OnIntegrateState after using the trigger value. The copy-modify-writeback pattern is required because Input is a value type on the context. Reusing Logic Across States States can share logic via internal static helper methods. For example, the JumpingState in the sample reuses FallingState.ComputeGravityAcceleration for gravity and MovingState.ComputeHorizontalMovementAcceleration for mid-air movement: public void OnIntegrateState(ref MyContext ctx) { // Reuse falling gravity computation FallingState.ComputeGravityAcceleration(ref ctx, terminalVelocity, out float3 gravityAccel); // Reuse moving horizontal acceleration MovingState.ComputeHorizontalMovementAcceleration( ref ctx.BaseContext, ctx.Profile.Moving, ctx.Input.MovementDirection, ctx.Input.MovementMagnitude, out float3 moveAccel); ctx.BaseContext.UpdateVelocitiesAndComputeDisplacements(moveAccel, gravityAccel); } This avoids duplicating physics logic while keeping each state self-contained. Common State Patterns Grounded State (Idle / Moving) OnEnterState: Zero out vertical velocity OnIntegrateState: Compute horizontal acceleration only Transitions: Check for jump input, loss of ground contact, slope above max Airborne State (Falling / Jumping) OnEnterState: Clear contact (LocomotionContact.None), optionally inherit platform velocity OnIntegrateState: Apply gravity + optional air control Transitions: Check for ground contact, wall contact Sliding State OnIntegrateState: Apply gravity (character slides down slope) Transitions: Check for loss of contact (to falling), ground contact below max slope (to moving/idle) Custom State Ideas Wall Running: Enter when jumping into a wall at speed; apply gravity slowly; allow wall jump Dashing: Enter on trigger; apply burst horizontal velocity; exit after timer Swimming: Enter when submerged; replace gravity with buoyancy; allow 3D movement Climbing: Enter at ladder; disable gravity; move along ladder axis Debugging States If your state isn't behaving as expected: Check transition priority order - an earlier transition might be firing first Log baseContext.LocomotionContact.ValueRO.ContactType to verify contact detection Verify that OnExitState and OnEnterState are called during transitions Ensure trigger inputs are accumulated in ApplyInputsToCharacter and consumed in OnIntegrateState Use the debug visualization to see contact points, movement paths, and impulses Next Steps The Execution Loop - When exactly each method is called Physics Configuration - Tuning capsule and collision parameters Recipe: Add a New State - Step-by-step guide to adding a state Best Practices - Recommended patterns for state logic
